source.title: Consensus-backed metadata rollup and reader-friendly changelog
source.author: Aisha Patel
source.release_notes: Add deterministic metadata rollup (consensus layer) that canonicalizes onboarding manifests and emits a condensed, reader-focused changelog for model releases. Includes heuristic merging for performance profiles and a lightweight conflict-resolution guide.
source.description:
Context and motivation: We onboard models from a variety of sources (author-uploaded manifests, CI-provided metadata, and discovery probes). Discrepancies between sources (missing fields, differing perf metrics, variant aliases) have caused noisy changelogs and occasional release regressions. This PR implements a deterministic "consensus rollup" step during intake that: 1) canonicalizes manifest keys and field formats; 2) merges performance profile vectors using a configurable aggregator (weighted median by signal confidence); 3) records provenance for each field so downstream systems can explain where a value came from; and 4) emits a concise, reader-friendly changelog "teaser" that groups changes into: metadata edits, perf deltas, and variant-level lifecycle events.

What changed (high level):
- New consensus rollup service (registry/rollup) that takes a set of manifests and returns a single canonical manifest plus a changelog fragment.
- Aggregator implementations: weighted_median (default) and fallback_mean (opt-in).
- Field provenance map: compact provenance stamps stored alongside canonical fields for traceability.
- Changelog formatter (registry/changelog/teaser) that outputs a short prose summary plus machine tags for UI tooling.
- Intake pipeline wiring: the onboarding task now calls rollup before persisting model versions.
- Tests: unit tests for canonicalization rules, aggregator edge cases, and an end-to-end integration test that runs a small sample intake and asserts changelog shape.

Commits (summary):
- 8a7b2c1: add consensus rollup service and initial aggregator APIs
- 7f92d4e: implement weighted_median aggregator and provenance stamps
- f03bd9a: changelog teaser formatter and grouping rules
- c4a1b2e: hook rollup into intake task; add integration test harness
- d2f4e51: docs: onboarding guide updates and examples
- 1b8fa6c: fix: handle variant alias collisions; add extra unit tests

Files touched (high level):
- registry/rollup/service.py (new)
- registry/rollup/aggregators.py (new)
- registry/changelog/teaser.py (new)
- registry/intake/handler.py (modified)
- tests/registry/test_rollup.py (new)
- tests/registry/integration_test_sample_intake.py (new)
- docs/registry/onboarding.md (modified)
- pkg/schemas/model_manifest.yaml (modified)
- scripts/sample_manifests/*.json (added examples)

Review conversation (high-level excerpt):
- Ben Carter: "Great direction. Please clarify the conflict-resolution priority list in the docs and add a test for missing confidence scores." (requested changes)
- Aisha Patel (author): "Added explicit priority table to docs and added a test that defaults missing confidence to 0.4. Also documented how to opt into fallback_mean." (addressed)
- Lina Morales: "Can we guarantee aggregator determinism across Python versions? Consider sorting keys before aggregation." (comment)
- Aisha Patel: "Added key sorting and a deterministic float reduction step; included a note and added a regression test: tests/registry/test_rollup.py::test_aggregator_determinism." (addressed)
- Tomas Ilyin: "LGTM after CI green; small nits inline about log level and a type hint." (approved)

CI summary: Initial run showed one flaky integration test (tests/registry/integration_test_sample_intake.py::test_end_to_end) failing on timeout in the mock persistence layer. I reran the job; subsequent run passed. All checks reported green: lint, unit tests, integration, and build. Coverage slightly decreased in an unrelated helper module (acceptable per ENG-1934).

Backward compatibility and risk: No user-facing API changes. The canonical manifest schema was minimally extended with an optional provenance map; schema validations accept the new optional field. Intake path is feature-flagged on rollout; default enabled for new onboardings. The rollup introduces deterministic merging rules to reduce churn in release notes and should decrease noise. If a downstream system depends on specific field values from a single source, we document how to opt-out and pin source priority.

Testing and validation: Unit tests cover aggregator edge cases (empty lists, single-source, conflicting confidences). Integration test runs sample intake with three synthetic manifests and asserts canonical output and a changelog fragment with expected grouped headings. Perf: aggregator runs in ~3–7ms for typical manifests (measured in CI microbenchmark script). Added alerts if rollup latency exceeds 200ms in production.

Docs and examples: docs/registry/onboarding.md updated with a short example showing input manifests and resulting teaser. Added scripts/sample_manifests to allow reproducing the integration test locally.

Merge outcome: Squash-merged to main on 2026-03-02 after approvals. Post-merge actions: 1) feature flag rollout started in staging; 2) we scheduled a small canary sweep to validate changelog quality on ~50 recent onboardings (ENG-2041).

Notes for release engineers: There is a small database migration to add the optional provenance column (backfilled as NULL). Migration script included: db/migrations/20260302_add_provenance_column.sql. Rollback is safe (column optional).
source.notes: Canary and staging rollout planned; migration is backward-compatible. Monitor changelog churn and aggregator error rate metric registry.rollup.errors.

source.title: Contextual release pruner and variant signal hash
source.author: Maya Patel
source.description: Problem: onboarding pages and release notes for model variants accumulate many low-signal items that hide deployment risks. This PR implements a contextual release pruner that selects a small, high-signal subset of changelog fragments and pairs it with a compact variant signal hash — a deterministic, size-bounded digest derived from sampled telemetry features — to help reviewers and downstream tools quickly assess runtime affinity. Key ideas: (1) Variant signal hash condenses anonymized telemetry-derived features (sequence length distribution quantiles, common token-prefixes, average KV cache hit hint) into a stable 64-char hex digest. The hash is produced from deterministic sketching of reservoir-sampled telemetry aggregates so it is reproducible across uploads and small enough for manifests. (2) Release pruner ranks candidate changelog fragments by a hybrid score (telemetry-weighted impact + semantic novelty) and applies a length and redundancy-aware pruning pass to produce a 3-item short summary and optional extended list. Implementation details: sketches and hash generation live in registry/lib/signals/hash_sketch.go; pruning engine is in services/release/pruner.go with a preview REST endpoint at api/release_preview.go. The uploader CLI gained --publish-short-release to enable the pruned output; the registry UI shows both short and full views. Privacy and safety: telemetry inputs are pre-aggregated, bucketed, and clipped; no raw traces are exported. Backwards compatibility: new fields are additive; older clients ignore short_release and variant_signal_hash. Rollout plan: feature-flagged behind feature_flags.release_pruner, internal pilot for two weeks, then opt-in for partners. Metrics from pilot: reviewer acceptance rate for onboarding briefs rose from 68% to 91% and average onboarding read-time decreased by 40%.

source.title: Introduce compact stability ledger and change-contextualizer for snapshot diffs
source.author: Anika Rao
source.description: Motivation and summary: Large snapshot onboarding produced verbose profile blobs and brittle release notes; this PR provides a compact, append-only 'stability ledger' designed to store sparse stability signals and provenance per snapshot plus a change contextualizer that synthesizes concise release briefs from ledger deltas and sampled eval signatures. Implementation highlights: ledger implementation in registry/ledger/stability.py introduces a tight binary+JSON entry format keyed by snapshot hash ribbon (sha256_base58). Each entry keeps stability_token (low/medium/high), a small latency_histogram_bucketed summary, token_error_rate as a fixed-point string, and a truncated provenance string; typical entry size is <1KB. The diff serializer at registry/snapshots/diff_serializer.py emits compact delta blobs containing tensor fingerprints, model config deltas, and profile delta summaries (throughput, p50, p95). The serializer supports streaming ingestion and resumable uploads and uses streamed sha256 fingerprinting with an LZ4 compression path. The contextualizer in tools/changelog/contextualizer.go reads ledger deltas and sampled eval signatures and generates prioritized 2-4 sentence briefs that call out regression windows, likely causes, and suggested mitigation actions (e.g., try q4 quantization or revert specific tokenizer changes). Migration and rollout: scripts/migrate_ledger.sh provides an idempotent offline compaction pass that converts legacy verbose snapshot metadata into ledger entries; it is safe to run on read-replicas and gated to registry-admin IAM. The feature is behind registry.features.compact_ledger and will be enabled first for model-registry-internal and a small customer cohort for two weeks before broad rollout. Backwards compatibility: new lightweight API endpoint /snapshots/{id}/ledger returns the compact ledger entry alongside existing full profile for opt-in clients; no breaking changes to existing endpoints. Performance and security considerations: entries are intentionally small to remain indexable in our metadata DB; serializer uses streamed hashing and LZ4 to reduce upload cost; provenance strings are sanitized and truncated to avoid PII, and migration requires elevated IAM. Tests and verification: unit tests added: tests/test_stability_ledger.py and tests/test_diff_serializer.py. Integration harness in benchmark-lab/fixtures/snapshot-churn simulates heavy snapshot churn and validates brief quality metrics. Commits: commit 9a1f2d8 adds ledger core and serializer, commit 3c4b7a1 adds contextualizer and go bindings, commit e2b6c55 adds migration script and tests. Files changed at a glance: registry/ledger/stability.py, registry/snapshots/diff_serializer.py, tools/changelog/contextualizer.go, scripts/migrate_ledger.sh, docs/registry/ledger.md, tests/test_stability_ledger.py, tests/test_diff_serializer.py, benchmark-lab/fixtures/snapshot-churn/seed.yaml, tools/ci/ledger_pipeline.yml, examples/onboard/compact-ledger-example.json, Makefile updates. Review discussion summary: Marcus requested deterministic fingerprinting and a terse entry schema; author clarified the streamed sha256 approach and added a compatibility shim. Sofia asked for privacy guarantees; added sanitization and a note in docs. Devon requested a rollout plan and metrics; added brief_quality and storage_savings metrics and a gated flag. CI: initial run showed a flaky integration test in the harness; rerun after increasing ingestion throttles passed all checks. Merge details: approved by reviewers and merged via squash-merge to main. Release notes: "Add compact stability ledger and contextual changelog generator for snapshot diffs; opt-in API endpoint /snapshots/{id}/ledger; migration script for converting legacy snapshot metadata; feature gated behind registry.features.compact_ledger." Rollout checklist: feature flag, enable internal cohort, monitor brief_quality >=0.85 and storage savings >=40% over legacy blobs, then enable for all. Additional notes: contextualizer is intentionally conservative and surfaces suggestions, not automated rollbacks; storage team consulted on index sizing and approved columnar entry plan.

CI: initial run showed a flaky integration test in the harness; rerun after increasing ingestion throttles passed all checks. Merge details: approved by reviewers and merged via squash-merge to main. Release notes: "Add compact stability ledger and contextual changelog generator for snapshot diffs; opt-in API endpoint /snapshots/{id}/ledger; migration script for converting legacy snapshot metadata; feature gated behind registry.features.compact_ledger." Rollout checklist: feature flag, enable internal cohort, monitor brief_quality >=0.85 and storage savings >=40% over legacy blobs, then enable for all. Additional notes: contextualizer is intentionally conservative and surfaces suggestions, not automated rollbacks; storage team consulted on index sizing and approved columnar entry plan.

source.title: Introduce mini-prober and cost-signature autodoc for lightweight model intake
source.author: Asha Raman
source.release_notes:
- Add a lightweight onboarding prober that collects sample usage stats and estimates per-request cost impact.
- Persist a compact 'cost-signature' into model metadata used by the release drafter to auto-compose changelog cost notes.
- Expose --mini-probe CLI flag and a feature-flagged rollout for private clusters.
source.body:
Context and motivation: Model onboarding at scale needs a lightweight way to capture representative cost signals (average sequence length, token distribution, cache-friendliness) without running full telemetry. This PR adds a small, safe prober that runs a bounded sampling workload during onboarding and produces a compact 'cost-signature' artifact. The platform uses the signature to offer conservative cost guidance and to insert an autodrafted changelog entry when a model is onboarded or updated.

What I changed (high level): Added a mini-prober library and CLI wiring, a cost-signature serializer embedded in model metadata, a release-drafter hook that uses the signature to create a simple cost blurb, and tests/fixtures. The prober is intentionally conservative: max 50 sample prompts, short sequence caps, no external dataset downloads. It runs only when --mini-probe is enabled or when the onboarding flow includes the 'probe' step.

Design notes: The cost-signature is a compact JSON line (stored base64 in metadata) with fields: avg_tokens_est, median_seq_len, kv-cache-hit-estimate, bucketed-token-dist, quant-suggest (none/8bit/4bit), and a heuristic cost-impact-class (low/medium/high). The heuristics are conservative and documented in scripts/onboard/README.md.

Files and areas touched: sources/onboard/mini_prober.py, cli/onboard.py (flag wiring), metadata/cost_signature.py, release/drafter/hooks/cost_autodraft.py, tests/test_mini_prober.py, docs/onboard/prober.md.

Testing and CI: Added unit tests for signature serialization, a small integration harness that runs in CI with mocked model weights, and a docs page. CI run passed all checks after a lint fix.

Rollout plan: Feature-flag in main; rollout gated behind feature 'mini_prober.experimental' and opt-in via CLI by default. After 2 weeks of telemetry and a manual review, flip the opt-in to recommended. Private installer will default to disabled.

Checklist:
- [x] Unit tests added
- [x] Integration harness added
- [x] Documentation in docs/onboard/prober.md
- [x] Release notes drafted and auto-attached

Merge: Squash-and-merge after CI green and at least two approvals.
commit_summary: mini-prober: add sampling harness and conservative heuristics
metadata: cost-signature serializer and base64 metadata writer
release: hook to autodraft cost blurb from cost-signature
source.review_thread:
Diego: Can we guarantee the prober won't leak data or run arbitrary remote requests?
Asha: The prober is sandboxed; it runs only on provided prompts or a small canned seed, network disabled in harness, no dataset pull. We added tests that assert no network calls.
Priya: Suggest adding a max-run-time to avoid long onboarding stalls.
Asha: Added timeout and early-exit after 50 samples in mini_prober.py.
Evan: Approve after lint fix and CI green.
Diego: Approved.
Priya: Approved.
source.notes_on_qa: QA ran the prober on a local small model image and confirmed the cost-signature fields are present in metadata and the autodraft hook produces the expected one-paragraph cost blurb. Load on CI runner measured <30s for the harness. Edge-case: when prompts are all extremely short, heuristic class can under-estimate; we added a floor clamp in signature computation.
source.changelog_excerpt: Added lightweight onboarding probe that writes a compact cost-signature into model metadata; release drafter will add cost guidance to model release notes.
source.security_notes: Prober runs with network disabled in CI and has a strict timeout; metadata is base64-encoded but not encrypted (customers can opt to remove signatures in private installs).
source.manual_rollback_instructions: Revert feature flag 'mini_prober.experimental' or remove cost-signature entries from metadata store for affected model versions.
follow_up_tasks: ENG-2030: add telemetry-backed calibration for cost heuristics
PM-119: collect customer feedback on cost guidance wording
DOC-55: expand prober documentation with examples

source.title: introduce-release-lighthouse-and-versioned-metrics-extractor
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: release notes generation has become a bottleneck during model onboarding. Teams repeatedly rewrite similar perf/context blurb templates and manually reconcile metric snapshots for each model variant. This PR introduces a "release lighthouse" extractor that synthesizes a candidate human-friendly release synopsis and a versioned-metrics artifact (time-stamped, normalized vectors) for each onboarding run so downstream docs and the Console can consume consistent, auditable release snippets.

Design summary: the feature adds a pipeline step (registry.ingest -> lighthouse.extract -> artifact.store) that: (1) samples keyed perf snapshots (latency P50/P95, tokens/sec, mem), (2) runs lightweight heuristics to surface regressions and noteworthy regressions, (3) generates a short prose synopsis with labeled highlights (throughput, footprint, notable regressions) and optional mitigation notes, and (4) emits a versioned JSON metrics artifact and a markdown candidate release blurb. The extractor is intentionally deterministic given the same sampling seed to make changelogs reproducible.

Checklist: - add extractor module and feature flag - persist versioned metrics under model artifact namespace - wire a Console preview endpoint for candidate blurb - add unit and integration tests for sampling, formatting, and schema validity - add Linear links and a short rollout plan

Testing and QA: unit tests cover sampling heuristics and formatters; integration tests run the pipeline with a small synthetic model profile and validate the produced artifact schema and a candidate blurb length/structure. Ran the full CI twice to catch flakiness in the perf-sampler; added a backoff in the sampler to address transient timeouts when contacting the benchmarks store. Staging rollout validated with a synthetic canary (ref: ENG-8132).

Backward compatibility & migration: produced metrics artifacts are versioned (v1 -> v2) with a compatibility shim in the registry reader so Console and downstream scripts continue to work. Consumers can opt in to the new prose blurb via a feature flag. No changes required to existing onboarding contracts.

Rollout plan: feature-flag gated on prod. First enable for internal curation only for two weeks, then open to all model intake flows. Add a follow-up PR to expose the blurb editor in the Console and to hook the artifact into release automation.

Related: ENG-8132 (release meta automation), ENG-9345 (perf sampling reliability), SUP-1213 (support ticket that motivated timeline).

Release notes (draft): Add release lighthouse: automatically generate candidate release synopses and versioned performance artifacts during model onboarding. Improves consistency of release notes and provides auditable metric snapshots.
commits: feat(lighthouse): add core extractor and sampling harness
feat(storage): versioned artifact writer and namespace plumbing
test(lighthouse): add unit tests for sampling heuristics
ci: increase sampler retry/backoff to reduce transient failures
docs: document feature flag and migration notes
chore: small refactor to registry reader for v2 artifact compatibility
review_comments: Miguel Santos (request changes): The sampler retry loop should cap exponential backoff to avoid long CI runs; can you add a ceiling?
Ava Chen (author): Added a cap to backoff and adjusted CI job timeout; updated tests to use a deterministic seed.
Priya Narayanan: Can you add a note about privacy (strip any sample that contains PII) in the README?
Ava Chen: Added a PII scrub step and a test to ensure sample payloads are sanitized before formatting.
Ethan Brooks (approve): Tests and docs look good; approving pending squash merge.
source.notes_for_sre: Sampler increases a small amount of background traffic to the benchmarks store when enabled; we added client-side rate limiting and retries. SRE should watch benchmarks-store error rate and onboarding latency for the first 48 hours post-rollout.
source.release_notes: Feature: release lighthouse - automatic candidate release synopses and versioned performance artifacts for model onboarding. Opt-in by feature flag; internal rollout first.
source.rollback_plan: Disable feature flag and delete v2 lighthouse artifacts for canary models if telemetry shows elevated error rate. Revert is safe since registry reader has compatibility shim.
source.human_readable_summary: Adds an automated step to synthesize release synopses and stable, versioned metric artifacts during model intake. Deterministic sampling and a migration shim preserve backward compatibility.

source.title: Introduce onboard lineage map and autorelease anchor synthesizer
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Problem: When onboarding models we lacked a machine-readable lineage artifact that connects model package uploads, profiling runs, and generated release notes. That made automated changelog synthesis brittle and required manual stitching for multi-variant releases. This PR adds two pieces: (1) a compact onboard lineage map that records upstream artifacts (source repo, dataset snapshot id, build hash, profiling snapshot id) and (2) an autorelease anchor synthesizer that produces a canonical release anchor and draft changelog entries based on lineage and profile diffs.

Design summary: The onboard lineage map is emitted as part of the onboarding pipeline and stored in the registry as a lightweight provenance document. It uses stable identifiers (dataset-snap, build-hash, profile-seed) and explicit relation types (built-from, profiled-by, variant-of). The autorelease anchor synthesizer consumes the lineage map and the model's performance profile snapshots to generate: a) a short human-facing release anchor (one-line summary), b) structured changelog notes (regressions, improvements, config changes), and c) a machine signal for the release gating pipeline. The synthesizer is conservative: it flags unknowns for manual review and emits confidence scores for each note.

Checklist implemented: 
- [x] Onboard step writes lineage map JSON (compact schema)
- [x] Registry API accepts lineage map and indexes provenance tags
- [x] Small migration to backfill lineage_uuid for recent uploads (best-effort)
- [x] Autorelease anchor synthesizer service + CLI hook (dry-run support)
- [x] Unit tests for synthesizer heuristics and profile-diff rules
- [x] E2E smoke with model-registry CI that runs onboard->synthesize flow

Migration notes: Backfill is best-effort for the last 90 days and writes lineage_uuid metadata on the model object when provenance can be inferred from upload artifacts and telemetry. If backfill fails, the synthesizer will mark the model as "no-lineage" and surface it to the curation queue. No user-visible API schema changes for existing clients; lineage is optional.

Testing / QA: Added a small set of synthetic onboard fixtures (3 variants) to exercise regressions detection (latency and MRR), and CI has an additional job that runs the synthesizer in dry-run mode and asserts output schema and non-empty anchors. Manual review by Marco and Sofia focused on heuristics thresholds and wording in generated notes.

Why this is safe: The changes are additive and guarded by feature flags (registry.feature.lineage and registry.feature.autorelease). The synthesizer never auto-promotes to a release; it only produces draft anchors unless the team flips the autorelease.allow_auto_publish flag.

Related: ENG-4021 (provenance roadmap), ENG-4090 (changelog automation spike).
commits: 894c2f9  add lineage map emitter and schema
a1b7d3e  implement registry API storage/indexing for lineage_uuid
c34f20b  synthesizer core: diff heuristic and template staging
de9a811  add CLI hook and dry-run mode
f2b6d44  tests: unit + e2e fixtures for onboard->synthesize flow
9d7e5aa  doc: onboarding migration and feature flag notes

@@ synthesizer/core/synth.py +88, -20
+def synth_anchor(lineage, profiles):
+    # compute perf diffs and generate concise anchor
+    return {"anchor": anchor_text, "notes": notes, "confidence": 0.86}
review_comments: Marco Ruiz: The heuristics look good; can we expose the latency threshold as a config var? Also suggest a more conservative default for patch releases.
Ava Chen (author): Accepted; added registry.config.synth.latency_threshold and lowered default for patch channels. Updated docs.
Sofia Li: Are we comfortable with the backfill window? Might introduce noise from old profiling artifacts.
Ava Chen: Backfill is gated and best-effort; added logging and an opt-out flag registry.backfill.enabled=false for customers that want zero mutation during migration.

Ellen Park: CI job failed once on flakey fixture; recommend increasing timeout.
Ellen Park: +1 after timeout bump and re-run.
CI Bot: synth-dryrun job passed on re-run (2026-02-18 06:02 UTC).
source.merge_outcome_notes: Feature flags enabled by default in staging only. Backfill migration runs as a separate safe job and is idempotent. Post-merge: ops run backfill job during a low-traffic window and monitor curation queue for 'no-lineage' hits.
source.release_notes: Added onboard lineage map (optional provenance) and an autorelease anchor synthesizer that drafts changelog anchors from lineage + profile diffs. Backfill available for recent uploads; feature controlled by flags.

source.title: add provenance-backed metadata enricher and performance-stamp publisher
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description: Motivation: When models are onboarded we lack a compact, auditable performance signature attached to the model metadata that can be consumed by release note automation and ops tooling. This PR introduces a provenance-backed metadata enricher that synthesizes canonical metadata fields (fingerprint, hardware-affinity hints, sample-derived latency/p90/cost stamps) and a separate performance-stamp publisher that emits a small, versioned artifact used by the changelog/autodraft hook to populate 'performance notes' in release drafts. Scope: 1) onboarding enricher service (onboard/enricher) that runs post-ingest to compute and attach a performance signature and provenance ledger entry; 2) publisher service (publisher/stamp_publisher) that signs and stores a compact JSON stamp in model artifacts storage and emits an event for release automation; 3) small CLI hook (hooks/autodraft_publisher) consumed by the changelog synthesizer to include the stamp in draft release notes; 4) unit + integration tests and docs. Design notes: the enricher uses existing sampling harness (ingest/probes) and a deterministic fingerprinting algorithm to ensure stamps are reproducible; the publisher attaches a provenance token and KMS-wrapped signature (optional via feature flag) so downstream workflows can assert authenticity. Backwards compatibility: stamps are optional metadata keys and will not block existing onboards. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout initially on the canary intake pipeline, then enabled by default after 2 weeks of telemetry. Checklist: - [x] core enricher implementation - [x] publisher that writes signed stamp to blobstore - [x] changelog hook to read stamps and synthesize a performance blurb - [x] unit and integration tests - [x] docs and runbook entry - [x] feature-flag gating and migration path Tests: added deterministic unit tests for fingerprinting and a small integration test that simulates probe ingestion and stamp emission. Security: signing flow is pluggable; by default uses a development key if KMS is not configured. Impact: improves fidelity of automated release notes and provides a verifiable performance artifact for platform teams. See related Linear items ENG-1428 and ENG-1492 for telemetry and rollout milestones.
commits: 1a2b3c4: introduce onboard enricher module and basic fingerprint algorithm
2b3c4d5: add performance-stamp publisher and storage client wrapper
3c4d5e6: add autodraft changelog hook to merge stamp into drafts
4d5e6f7: tests: unit and integration harness for enricher + publisher
5e6f7g8: docs: onboarding metadata enricher and rollout runbook
6f7g8h9: feature-flag and migration utilities + telemetry counters

title: add provenance-backed metadata enricher and performance-stamp publisher
preview: Motivation: When models are onboarded we lack a compact, auditable performance signature attached to the model metadata that can be consumed by release note automation and ops tooling. This PR introduces a provenance-backed metadata enricher that synthesizes canonical metadata fields (fingerprint, hardware-affinity hints, sample-derived latency/p90/cost stamps) and a separate performance-stamp publisher that emits a small, versioned artifact used by the changelog/autodraft hook to populate 'performance notes' in release drafts. Scope: 1) onboarding enricher service (onboard/enricher) that runs post-ingest to compute and attach a performance signature and provenance ledger entry; 2) publisher service (publisher/stamp_publisher) that signs and stores a compact JSON stamp in model artifacts storage and emits an event for release automation; 3) small CLI hook (hooks/autodraft_publisher) consumed by the changelog synthesizer to include the stamp in draft release notes; 4) unit + integration tests and docs. Design notes: the enricher uses existing sampling harness (ingest/probes) and a deterministic fingerprinting algorithm to ensure stamps are reproducible; the publisher attaches a provenance token and KMS-wrapped signature (optional via feature flag) so downstream workflows can assert authenticity. Backwards compatibility: stamps are optional metadata keys and will not block existing onboards. Rollout plan: feature-flagged rollout initially on the canary intake pipeline, then enabled by default after 2 weeks of telemetry. Checklist: - [x] core enricher implementation - [x] publisher that writes signed stamp to blobstore - [x] changelog hook to read stamps and synthesize a performance blurb - [x] unit and integration tests - [x] docs and runbook entry - [x] feature-flag gating and migration path Tests: added deterministic unit tests for fingerprinting and a small integration test that simulates probe ingestion and stamp emission. Security: signing flow is pluggable; by default uses a development key if KMS is not configured. Impact: improves fidelity of automated release notes and provides a verifiable performance artifact for platform teams. See related Linear items ENG-1428 and ENG-1492 for telemetry and rollout milestones.

source.title: Rollup compat-map and onboarding metadata adapter with changelog seed backfill
source.author: Samir Patel
release_notes: Add compat-map rollup (aggregates pairwise compatibility into release-ready matrix)
Introduce onboarding metadata adapter to normalize provider and variant metadata for downstream tooling
Backfill changelog seeds for 48 recent model onboardings (non-destructive migration)
Performance: ~12% faster profile lookup in registry index during release assembly
source.description:
Motivation: We have overlapping compatibility entries across model variants that complicate release note generation and rollout heuristics. This PR introduces a compatibility rollup step that composes pairwise compat entries into a compact matrix and a lightweight metadata adapter that normalizes incoming onboard payloads (provider, quantization tags, variant hints) so downstream profile generation and changelog seeds are stable.

What this PR does: - Adds a compat-rollup module that deduplicates and collapses pairwise compat rules into a hierarchical matrix keyed by model family/version. - Adds metadata-adapter layer which runs as a normalization pass during onboarding and when re-ingesting legacy manifests. - Adds a changelog-seed backfill migration that synthesizes short human-friendly blurbs for recent onboardings so release assembler has content to surface. - Adds unit + integration tests for rollup correctness and performance regression guard.

Checklist: - [x] compat-rollup algorithm implemented and unit-tested - [x] metadata adapter schema + validator - [x] changelog seed migration + idempotency guard - [x] e2e test with release-assembler (mocked) - [x] perf microbenchmark against registry index

Migration notes: The backfill is non-destructive: it writes changelog seeds to the registry only when no seed exists. The migration is safe to run repeatedly. We added a short TTL for temporary debug flags and a dry-run mode for the migration to inspect candidate seeds. Operators should run the dry-run once, inspect output, then run the actual migration during a low-traffic window.

Testing: Unit tests cover edgecases (circular compat rules, missing variant metadata). Integration test simulates onboard -> rollup -> release-assembler flow and asserts stable release_notes output. Local perf runs show ~12% reduction in profile lookup latency for heavy variant graphs (N=200 variants).

Related: ENG-4981 contains design notes on the rollup heuristics, SUP-1123 is the support ticket that prompted standardized metadata for third-party picks.

If anything goes wrong: rollback is a two-step process: 1) disable the migration flag to stop backfills; 2) deploy a revert that bypasses the rollup and falls back to the previous pairwise compat API.
source.commits: 3 commits: (1) compat: add rollup algorithm + tests; (2) adapter: onboarding metadata normalizer + schema; (3) migration: changelog seed backfill + dry-run tooling
review_comments: Ava Chen: Please add a short note in ops docs about the dry-run output format — where do operators look for sample seeds?
Samir Patel: Added docs/ops/runbackfill.md and sample output; addressed in latest commit.
Diego Martinez: Algorithm looks good. We should ensure the rollup maintains transitive closure invariants; add a test for chained constraints.
Samir Patel: Added test for chained constraints and clarified invariant in docs.
Priya Nambiar: LGT M with minor nits on logging level and a suggestion to make the migration idempotency check optimistic.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash by Ava Chen after approvals. No post-merge hotfix required.

source.title: curation-playbook-and-changelog-translation-hook
source.author: Jonah Fogel
source.description:
Motivation: release notes and compact changelogs generated by the model registry are currently English-first and optimized for machine consumption. Customers and internal stakeholders have requested two things: 1) better human-readable, concise release blurbs that can be surfaced in the Console and email digests; 2) localized (i18n) variants of those blurbs so non-English engineering and product teams can review and approve model rollouts without translation bottlenecks. This PR introduces a lightweight curation playbook and a changelog translation/templating hook that sit in the onboard -> release-assembler pipeline.

What this PR does (high level):
- Adds a curation playbook module (src/curation/playbook.py) that codifies checks and heuristics used during model onboarding: perf delta thresholds, sample-based sanity checks, affinity/quantization compatibility hints, and human-review triggers. The playbook emits a small JSON-compatible report used by the release assembler.
- Adds changelog translation hook (src/release/translation_hook.py) which takes a canonical compact-change object and renders both: (a) short human blurbs (1-2 sentence, bullet-safe) and (b) localized renderings using a glossary and simple grammar templates. The translation hook is intentionally offline-friendly and deterministic (no external translation API calls at build time).
- Integrates the playbook/translation-hook into the release assembler pipeline (src/release/assembler.py) and exposes a new CLI command redwood-model-registry release preview --locale <locale> to generate localized preview snippets for reviewers.
- Adds sample-driven annotations: small sample sets (tests/fixtures/samples) and an efficient sampler that runs in CI to detect semantic regressions for onboarding candidates (if the semantic similarity drops > threshold we mark for manual review).
- Adds unit + integration tests and a small e2e preview job in .github/workflows/release-preview.yml that renders localized blurbs in CI for PRs touching model metadata or changelog templates.

Checklist (what I validated):
- [x] Playbook rules implemented and unit-tested
- [x] Translation templates + glossary + locale fallback implemented
- [x] CLI preview and integration with assembler
- [x] End-to-end test for a toy model onboarding -> preview flow
- [x] Docs: added short README and usage notes under docs/curation

Notes on design choices:
- We intentionally avoid a black-box MT step in CI; templates + glossary yield consistent, reviewable outputs. Teams can plug in managed MT later via a small adapter.
- Playbook emits a concise report (score + flags) rather than gating merges; gating is left to retention policies and human reviewers.
- The compatibility hints are heuristic (quant/affinity scoring) and are surfaced as hints (not enforced), since hardware mapping varies per customer deployment.

Related work: ENG-5421 (model release localization) and PM-88 (concise changelog UX). This PR is intentionally self-contained and additive to the assembler; it introduces no breaking API changes.
commits: 4eab12c - add curation playbook scaffolding and basic rule set
a7d5f10 - implement translation hook and template renderer
c0213aa - integrate playbook output into release assembler and CLI preview
b9f7d88 - add sample-based sanity sampler and fixtures
f2d8a33 - tests: add unit + integration + e2e preview workflow
d3c6b01 - docs: curation README and usage examples; fix nits
review_comments: Ava Chen (2026-01-18): Nice decomposition of playbook rules. Two asks: 1) please add a short examples table in the README showing a failing sample and how the playbook flags it; 2) can you make the CLI return non-zero when the preview contains >1 manual-review flags?
Jonah Fogel (2026-01-19): Addressed both: added examples to docs and made CLI exit 2 when manual-review flags > 0 (so it can be used in gating scripts). See commit b9f7d88.

Diego Martínez (2026-01-21): The translation templates look good but the glossary needs an allowlist for model-specific tokens (e.g., <MASK>, <EOS>) to avoid accidental translation. Also requested a test covering a right-to-left locale.
Jonah Fogel (2026-01-22): Added token-allowlist and RTL test (tests/test_translation_hook.py::test_rtl_preview). Commit c0213aa.
Sara Patel (2026-01-25): CI showed a flake in the sampler e2e job (time budget). Please add a small timeout and retry on sampler network ops.
Jonah Fogel (2026-01-27): Added retry/backoff to sampler and adjusted e2e job budget; re-ran CI and green. Commit d3c6b01.
ci_log:
CI run 2026-01-23: unit tests fail (timeout in sampler e2e) — investigation pinned to network call in sampler
CI run 2026-01-27: all checks passed (unit + integration + release-preview workflow)
source.release_notes: Add curation playbook that produces onboarding quality reports and a translation hook that renders concise human-friendly and localized changelog snippets. Includes a CLI preview command and CI preview workflow. Improves reviewability for non-English stakeholders; no breaking changes.
notes: This PR intentionally keeps templates deterministic to make review easier; a follow-up will add an adapter for managed translation services (ENG-5580).
Operational impact: the release preview workflow increases CI run time slightly (~+30s per affected PR) but remains optional via label opt-in.
Follow-ups: integration with Console preview UI (PM-102) and a user-facing localization pipeline (ENG-5580).

source.title: Expose canonical model+version identifiers for runtime telemetry
source.author: Quentin Harris
source.description:
Context: perf-canary baselines and alerting are keyed per model. Today, runtime telemetry frequently emits an unstable identifier (human alias, internal deployment name, or a resolved artifact pointer) depending on routing and control-plane config. That causes baseline fragmentation (same model -> multiple time series) and false regressions during rollouts or alias updates. This PR adds a canonical, registry-owned model identifier to the Model Registry "resolve" response and ensures the identifier is suitable for telemetry and long-lived aggregation.

What this changes: 1) Introduces canonical identifiers: model_id (stable UUID), model_slug (stable string, e.g. "llama-3-8b-instruct"), and model_version (registry semantic version or registry version id) as first-class fields in registry responses. 2) Extends the ResolveModel API to return canonical_model and canonical_version alongside the artifact reference used for loading. 3) Adds a dedicated "telemetry_key" (string) intended for metrics labels: "<model_slug>@<model_version>" (e.g. "llama-3-8b-instruct@2025-11-15.1"). This is explicitly NOT a customer-facing name; it is guaranteed stable for the lifetime of a model version in the registry. 4) Adds audit logging when alias -> canonical mapping changes, to aid post-incident for any baseline shifts.

Compatibility and rollout: - Backwards compatible: existing fields remain unchanged. New fields are additive. - The intended consumer is runtime telemetry (see redwood PR-19193 / PR-19203 follow-ups). Runtime can start emitting telemetry_key immediately once it updates its client. - The telemetry_key is designed to be low-cardinality: slug + version only. No region, hardware, tier, customer, or deployment id.

Why this solves baseline stability: perf-canary baselines are stored as {model, version, runtime_version, sku, region?}. If "model" shifts between alias vs internal deployment name across rollouts, baselines drift or reset. Canonical slug@version ensures series continuity across routing changes while still differentiating real version updates.

Notes: - model_slug is immutable after a model is published (enforced in registry). If we ever need to rename, we will publish a new slug and treat it as a new model for baseline purposes. - model_version is immutable; version string is validated and normalized. - We purposely do not include quantization profile or runtime kernel tags here; those belong to runtime telemetry (and are handled in separate PRs).

Testing: - Added unit tests for version normalization and immutability constraints. - Added integration test for ResolveModel returning canonical fields through the public registry client. - Load-tested label generation to ensure we do not allocate on hot paths (string concat is done once per resolve).

Follow-ups: - Runtime adoption PR in redwood (emit telemetry_key as the canonical model label). - Backfill job: map historical alias-based series to canonical where possible (telemetry pipeline).
commits: model-registry: add canonical model/version structs and serialization
api: extend ResolveModel response with canonical_model + telemetry_key
validation: enforce immutability of model_slug and model_version after publish
audit: log alias updates (old->new) with canonical targets
tests: resolve integration + version normalization cases
docs: update registry client README and API changelog
review_conversation: Omar El-Sayed: Can you confirm this is stable across alias changes + routing? We saw metrics label flips when the control plane swapped aliases during the canary rollout.

Quentin Harris (author): Yes. telemetry_key is derived from canonical slug + canonical version, both immutable post-publish. Aliases only point to canonical targets and can change without affecting telemetry_key.
Nadia Rahman: Please add a note about cardinality and explicitly forbid including deployment id / customer tags in this key. We have a metrics-cardinality audit running for perf-canary.
Quentin Harris (author): Added docs/telemetry-identifiers.md with explicit rules + examples. Also added a unit test ensuring the generated key does not include alias/deployment fields.
Logan Wright: Minor: any chance version strings come through inconsistent ("v1" vs "1" vs build metadata)? We need strict normalization for grouping.

Quentin Harris (author): Implemented normalization + validation: accepted formats are YYYY-MM-DD.N for our curated models and semver for OSS. Stored normalized; rejected if it would change after publish. Added tests for common mismatches.
Rina Kobayashi: For perf-canary, we’ll need to resolve and pin the version used in the workload definition. Does ResolveModel now return the exact published version (not "latest")?
Quentin Harris (author): Yes. ResolveModel returns canonical_version even if the request is "latest"; callers can log/emit that resolved version for telemetry. That’s the intended behavior.
Omar El-Sayed: Approving contingent on keeping this additive (no breaking proto changes).
Quentin Harris (author): Confirmed additive-only. proto check in CI is green.
All reviewers: Approved.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main
source.release_notes: Model Registry ResolveModel now returns canonical model and version identifiers (including telemetry_key = <model_slug>@<model_version>) for stable runtime telemetry and perf-canary baselines. Additive change; no breaking API removals.

If "model" shifts between alias vs internal deployment name across rollouts, baselines drift or reset. Canonical slug@version ensures series continuity across routing changes while still differentiating real version updates.

Notes: - model_slug is immutable after a model is published (enforced in registry). If we ever need to rename, we will publish a new slug and treat it as a new model for baseline purposes. - model_version is immutable; version string is validated and normalized. - We purposely do not include quantization profile or runtime kernel tags here; those belong to runtime telemetry (and are handled in separate PRs).

Testing: - Added unit tests for version normalization and immutability constraints. - Added integration test for ResolveModel returning canonical fields through the public registry client. - Load-tested label generation to ensure we do not allocate on hot paths (string concat is done once per resolve).

Follow-ups: - Runtime adoption PR in redwood (emit telemetry_key as the canonical model label). - Backfill job: map historical alias-based series to canonical where possible (telemetry pipeline).
text:
Context: perf-canary baselines and alerting are keyed per model. Today, runtime telemetry frequently emits an unstable identifier (human alias, internal deployment name, or a resolved artifact pointer) depending on routing and control-plane config. That causes baseline fragmentation (same model -> multiple time series) and false regressions during rollouts or alias updates. This PR adds a canonical, registry-owned model identifier to the Model Registry "resolve" response and ensures the identifier is suitable for telemetry and long-lived aggregation.

What this changes: 1) Introduces canonical identifiers: model_id (stable UUID), model_slug (stable string, e.g. "llama-3-8b-instruct"), and model_version (registry semantic version or registry version id) as first-class fields in registry responses. 2) Extends the ResolveModel API to return canonical_model and canonical_version alongside the artifact reference used for loading. 3) Adds a dedicated "telemetry_key" (string) intended for metrics labels: "<model_slug>@<model_version>" (e.g. "llama-3-8b-instruct@2025-11-15.1"). This is explicitly NOT a customer-facing name; it is guaranteed stable for the lifetime of a model version in the registry. 4) Adds audit logging when alias -> canonical mapping changes, to aid post-incident for any baseline shifts.

Compatibility and rollout: - Backwards compatible: existing fields remain unchanged. New fields are additive. - The intended consumer is runtime telemetry (see redwood PR-19193 / PR-19203 follow-ups). Runtime can start emitting telemetry_key immediately once it updates its client. - The telemetry_key is designed to be low-cardinality: slug + version only. No region, hardware, tier, customer, or deployment id.

Why this solves baseline stability: perf-canary baselines are stored as {model, version, runtime_version, sku, region?}. If "model" shifts between alias vs internal deployment name across rollouts, baselines drift or reset. Canonical slug@version ensures series continuity across routing changes while still differentiating real version updates.

Notes: - model_slug is immutable after a model is published (enforced in registry). If we ever need to rename, we will publish a new slug and treat it as a new model for baseline purposes. - model_version is immutable; version string is validated and normalized. - We purposely do not include quantization profile or runtime kernel tags here; those belong to runtime telemetry (and are handled in separate PRs).

Testing: - Added unit tests for version normalization and immutability constraints. - Added integration test for ResolveModel returning canonical fields through the public registry client. - Load-tested label generation to ensure we do not allocate on hot paths (string concat is done once per resolve).

Follow-ups: - Runtime adoption PR in redwood (emit telemetry_key as the canonical model label). - Backfill job: map historical alias-based series to canonical where possible (telemetry pipeline).

source.title: introduce-cohort-sample-manifests-and-watermark-backed-change-drafter
source.author: Maya Chen
source.description: Motivation: onboarding teams asked for smaller, targeted sample sets for release notes and regressions that map to customer cohorts (large-enterprise, mobile-first, helpdesk). In addition we need a way to produce concise changelog drafts that preserve sample provenance but remove identifying tokens: a watermark-backed drafting flow that ties a compact diff summary to a sampled manifest. Design: add a CohortSample manifest (schema v2) that declares sample cohorts, deterministic seed, and retention/privacy tags; implement a watermark drafters subsystem that can generate compact changelog teasers (one-line impact bullets + representative sample snippets) tied to a sample manifest hash and signature. The drafter uses K-anonymized snippet trimming and an optional token-redaction pipeline. Implementation: 1) manifest schema and validation (manifest/v2) + CLI preview (onboard-sample preview --manifest-file). 2) sampling worker that generates cohort-specific sample packs and stores lightweight fingerprints to the model registry storage backend. 3) watermark drafters service that consumes a manifest hash and emits a compact markdown-ready changelog snippet, with an attached provenance token for audit and rollback tooling. 4) compatibility checks added to the onboard pipeline: sample-aware performance baseline query and a lightweight eval probe that runs up to 100 queries across cohorts. 5) telemetry hooks so Console can show per-cohort sample coverage and digest generation latency. Rollout: behind feature-flag onboard.cohort_samples + gradual opt-in for model curators. Docs: CLI docs, manifest schema reference, and a short how-to for product teams to craft cohorts. Security/privacy: default redaction on snippets, signing of manifest hashes, and ability to opt-out of storing raw snippets. This PR groups the work into a logical change set to enable concise, cohort-aware release notes and safer public previews.
source.commits_summary: 6 commits: add manifest schema v2 and validator; sampling worker and deterministic seed; watermark drafter service and unit tests; CLI preview; registry API hooks and small telemetry additions; changelog examples and docs
source.review_conversation: Initial review requested more explicit privacy defaults and stronger validation for deterministic seeds. I (author) pushed commit 3 to enforce seed ranges and add a manifest-level 'privacy: redact_by_default' boolean. Priya requested a telemetry opt-out flag; added 'manifest.telemetry.disabled' and updated docs. Diego asked for tighter unit tests around the drafter's redaction (edge cases: email-like tokens, long numeric strings) and suggested adding deterministic sampling tests; added those tests in commit 5. Liam asked about performance cost of the cohort probe; we added a cap (100 queries) and a circuit-breaker. Approvals: Priya approved on 2025-11-24, Diego approved after tests passed, Liam approved after perf verification.
source.release_notes: Add cohort sample manifests (schema v2) and a watermark-based changelog drafter. Enables per-cohort concise release notes, deterministic sampling for reproducible regressions, and privacy-safe teaser generation. Controlled rollout behind onboard.cohort_samples feature flag.
source.testing_notes: Unit tests added for manifest validation and drafter redaction. End-to-end pipeline tested locally with a small model snapshot. Perf probe capped at 100 queries per manifest; CI runs an integration scenario using a synthetic sample pack. Manual QA: verified CLI preview output, manifest signature validation, and Console telemetry panel entries.
source.perf_impact: Read path unchanged for serving. Onboard pipeline: sampling job adds ~3% CPU on the curator worker during generation windows, storage footprint increased by ~120KB per sampled manifest (fingerprints + metadata). Drafter generation latency ~120-250ms per manifest on a small instance; negligible for UX flows.
source.rollout_plan: 1) Enable feature-flag for internal curators. 2) Run opt-in pilot with two product teams for 2 weeks. 3) Monitor telemetry: manifest-generation latency, sample-coverage, and any redaction failures. 4) Gradual enablement for all curators with 48h rollback path and a flag to disable public snippet previews.
source.notes_for_changelog_automation: Drafter emits a stable teaser payload that the changelog assembler can consume; payload contains manifest_hash, short impact bullets, and anonymized sample snippets. The assembler will now prefer cohort teasers when available and fall back to existing delta summarizers.
source.audit_log: manifest:v2 created (hash: abc123... truncated) by maya.chan@redwood on 2025-11-12; drafter run id: drafter-20251124-17a; signatures verified. Sensitive content redaction flag set to true by default.
source.authors_notes: This change was intentionally split to keep the sampler and drafter logically separated so that downstream release assembly can consume either component independently. Follow-up work: add compact coverage badges in Console and auto-schedule cohort-based evictions for stale samples.

source.title: Introduce shingled onboard metadata and size-aware release extract
source.author: Maya Rodriguez
source.description:
Background and motivation: onboarding very large model metadata bundles (profiles, test vectors, long-form provenance) has repeatedly caused large PR payloads and slow downstream release note generation. Large deltas were both noisy and expensive to store/serialize, and the release assembler often generated verbose changelogs that made it hard for release owners to triage. This PR introduces a shingled metadata serializer for onboard payloads (metadata shingling) and a size-aware release extract pipeline that samples and compresses changelog candidates to produce concise release notes. Goals: reduce payload size during model onboarding, make changelog summaries readable by humans, and provide deterministic, auditable release extracts for downstream automation.

What I changed:
- onboard/serializer: add shingle_encoder.py (shingled metadata encoding). The encoder splits large metadata maps into consistent "shingles" (content-addressed shards) keyed by a stable fingerprint. Shingles are stored alongside a compact manifest that references shingles with offsets and versioning metadata. This enables small delta uploads when only parts of the metadata change.
- onboard/cli: add --shingle-threshold flag to onboard CLI (defaults to 64KB per property) and a dry-run mode that emits a predicted shard manifest.
- registry/storage: add ShingleStore abstraction and an S3-backed implementation with reference-counted shard lifecycle and garbage-collection hooks. The ShingleStore exposes a small API used by the onboard pipeline to dedupe repeated payloads across models and versions.
- release/extract: add size_aware_changelog_sampler which reads profile deltas and picks representative metric changes with heuristics tuned to shard-level diffs. The sampler produces a prioritized list of up to N blurbs (configurable) and simulates a human-friendly sentence for each blurb.
- release/cli: add release-extract --summarize flag that creates a concise release_notes.json alongside the full changelog bundle.
- tests: unit tests for shingle_encoder edge cases, integration for full onboard flow (small model and large model with repeated metadata), and e2e test for release-extract summarization.
- docs: onboarding.md adds a section on shingling and guidance for when to enable it (enterprise customers with very large provenance).

Backwards compatibility: the shingled upload falls back to a legacy full-payload path when the registry is in compatibility mode; the manifest contains a version tag so older registries ignore shards until migrated. ShingleStore provides a compatibility loader that can rehydrate full metadata for older code paths.

Performance notes: local benchmarks included in this PR show ~3x reduction in upload bytes (median) for large profiles and a 40-60% reduction in changelog size for profiles with repetitive provenance entries. These numbers vary by profile shape; the sampler is conservative by default.

Security and cost: storing shingles increases object count in cloud storage; we added a GC policy and reference counting to avoid unbounded growth. Shingles are content-addressed and immutable; they respect existing KMS-encryption controls.

Migration:
- rollout plan: enable shingling behind a feature-flag; start with internal clusters and a small set of customers; monitor object growth and GC effectiveness.
- migration helper: a one-off migrator to pre-shingle the last N model versions for customers who opt-in.

Checklist:
- [x] Shingle encoder + tests
- [x] ShingleStore S3 impl + GC hooks
- [x] CLI flags and dry-run
- [x] Release sampler + summarizer
- [x] Unit/integration/e2e tests
- [x] Docs and upgrade notes
- [x] Basic perf benchmarks

How to review: focus on shingle encoding invariants (stable fingerprints across runs), GC safety (refcount correctness), and release-extract prioritization heuristics (are we omitting important regressions?).

Relevant Linear tickets: ENG-1742 (shingled metadata RFC) and ENG-1793 (concise changelog UX). Related work: this complements but does not replace the compact-profile delta machinery in profile-diff; the goal is orthogonal (store-time dedupe + human-friendly extracts).

d4e5f6a: implement ShingleStore S3 backend and refcount GC
b7c8d9f: CLI flags, dry-run output, and release-extract summarizer
c0d1e2f: add unit + integration tests for shingling and sampler
e3f4a5b: docs: onboarding.md updates and rollout notes

+    ...

+class ShingleStoreS3(ShingleStore):

+        # upload to s3://<bucket>/shingles/<fingerprint>
+        ...

review_comments: Ethan Park: Nice approach — please ensure the shingle fingerprint includes model_schema_version so we don't accidentally dedupe across incompatible manifests.
Maya Rodriguez: Good point; updated encoder to include schema version in fingerprint generation (see latest commit).
Lina Cho: GC looks OK at glance but add a safety-window before immediate deletion and expose a metrics counter for 'orphan_shingles' so SRE can watch.
Maya Rodriguez: Added safety_window_days=14 and an orphan_shingles metric to ShingleStore; added an integration test simulating interrupted GC.
Ravi Menon: Sampler heuristics are promising; suggest we add a low-threshold path for security/regression flags so critical metric drops are never omitted even if below the default score.

CI bot: rerun passed. All checks green.
Lina Cho: Approved after verifying GC metrics and the dry-run output on staging.
Ethan Park: Approved. Verified fingerprint stability across three deterministic runs with different property ordering.
Ravi Menon: Approved. Sampled release-extract output looked concise and captured the top regressions.

source.release_notes: Adds a shingled metadata serializer for onboard payloads to reduce upload size and enable deduping; introduces a size-aware changelog sampler that produces concise release notes (configurable). Backwards compatible; rollout behind feature flag. See docs/onboarding.md for migration and GC guidance.
source.extra_notes: Monitoring: added shingle_store.shards_total, shingle_store.orphan_shards, release_sampler.blurbs_generated. Rollout plan: gradual opt-in followed by a 30-day monitoring period. DB migrations: none. Storage impact: increases object count but reduces bytes uploaded per onboard; GC policy required.
source.notes_for_release_manager: Use release-extract --summarize to produce release_notes.json for the release PR; verify 'blurbs_generated' count in staging before enabling for customers.
source.testing_summary: Unit tests: 42 passed. Integration tests: 6 passed. E2E: 1 pass on staging with large-profile fixture. Benchmarks: local harness shows median upload bytes reduced ~3x for large profiles; changelog size reduced 40-60% for repetitive provenance patterns.

source.title: Introduce workload-conditioned profile compositor and changelog preview generator
source.author: Asha Patel
source.description:
Motivation: model onboarding currently produces multiple performance profiles from different probes (latency, token throughput, memory watermark) and from targeted synthetic workloads, but downstream consumers (serving, cost estimator, release notes) need a single coherent profile per variant. Manual merging is error-prone and hides important workload-dependent behavior. This PR adds a workload-conditioned profile compositor that deterministically merges multiple probe-derived profiles into a set of workload-conditioned variants (chat, summarization, long-context, embedding-heavy). It also introduces a changelog preview generator that synthesizes concise release notes describing the net change introduced by a new composite profile relative to the last released profile. The generated changelog is intended as a review artifact for curation and release ops.

What this change does: implements a compositing pipeline that (1) ingests probe profiles and workload histograms, (2) assigns workload buckets using a small configurable taxonomy, (3) computes weighted aggregates of latency/throughput percentiles and token-cost projections, (4) emits provenance metadata (input probes, seed hash, aggregation weights) and stability scores, and (5) writes composite profiles into the registry. The pipeline runs as a staged job in onboard-worker with safe idempotency keys. The changelog previewer computes deltas (absolute and relative), highlights potential regressions for the top-3 customer workload buckets, and produces a human-readable preview snippet saved alongside the profile draft.

Backward compatibility and opt-out: composition is opt-in via the onboard manifest field "compose_profiles: true". If absent, onboarding preserves current behavior. We added a global feature flag (registry config) to enable automatic composition for accounts that want it.

Testing and validation: unit tests for the aggregator, property tests to ensure deterministic results for identical inputs, integration tests that run small synthetic workloads through the full ingest->compose->publish flow, and an e2e smoke test that exercises changelog preview generation. CI initially exposed a flaky fixture in the ranking comparator; tests were adjusted to be time-insensitive and the CI run was re-triggered.

Rollout and migration notes: composite profiles are created as new variant objects (suffix "-composed"), not by mutating existing variants. This enables safe rollout and easy rollback. A follow-up task is tracked in ENG-11789 to add an automatic canary that will route 10% of traffic to the composed variant for a week before full promotion.

Related work: builds on earlier probe ingestion work (ENG-11234) and complements the release note automation pipeline.

Summary: adds workload-conditioned composition, deterministic provenance sealing, changelog preview generation, feature flag, tests, and docs for operators.
commits: 15c92d2 - introduce profile compositor core and workload taxonomy
e4b7a12 - add provenance stamping and deterministic seed generation
b9d3f8c - changelog previewer: delta extraction and humanized snippet builder
6f0a1c3 - onboard manifest: add compose_profiles flag and validation
a6d2b49 - integration tests for compose path and e2e smoke
c3d9e01 - docs: operator guide and feature flag notes
f8b41ea - test: make ranking comparator resilient to timing flakiness
review_comments: Jon Rivera: please add an explicit schema migration note; how will downstream consumers discover the new '-composed' suffix?
Asha Patel (author): added docs/operators/composed-profiles.md and registry change that exposes variant_aliases; left a comment in profile_publish to support alias discovery at read time.
Priya Natarajan: aggregation math looks good but can we surface percentile sample counts in the provenance metadata? Otherwise small-sample percentiles may be misleading.
Asha Patel (author): added sample_count fields to provenance and a short note in the changelog preview about low-sample warnings.
Evan Brooks: tests are comprehensive; had one nit about the template wording in composed_preview_template.md

Asha Patel (author): pushed wording tweak per Evan's suggestion and updated the template tests to assert trimmed whitespace.
CI: initial run had a failing test (ranking comparator) due to time sensitivity; I adjusted to use deterministic timestamps and re-ran. All checks passed on re-run.
source.release_notes: - Added workload-conditioned profile composition (opt-in via compose_profiles); - Changelog preview generator that synthesizes delta summaries for curator review; - Emitted provenance metadata (seed hash, input probes, weights, sample_counts); - New docs: operators/composed-profiles.md; - Safe rollout via new '-composed' variant suffix.
source.notes_for_release: Ops: to enable auto composition globally set registry.feature_flags.compose_profiles=true. Existing onboard manifests without the field are unchanged. If you rely on variant name stability, watch for '-composed' suffix behavior and update any hard-coded consumers.
source.merge_outcome: merged to main after approvals and green CI

title: Introduce workload-conditioned profile compositor and changelog preview generator
preview:
Motivation: model onboarding currently produces multiple performance profiles from different probes (latency, token throughput, memory watermark) and from targeted synthetic workloads, but downstream consumers (serving, cost estimator, release notes) need a single coherent profile per variant. Manual merging is error-prone and hides important workload-dependent behavior. This PR adds a workload-conditioned profile compositor that deterministically merges multiple probe-derived profiles into a set of workload-conditioned variants (chat, summarization, long-context, embedding-heavy). It also introduces a changelog preview generator that synthesizes concise release notes describing the net change introduced by a new composite profile relative to the last released profile. The generated changelog is intended as a review artifact for curation and release ops.

What this change does: implements a compositing pipeline that (1) ingests probe profiles and workload histograms, (2) assigns workload buckets using a small configurable taxonomy, (3) computes weighted aggregates of latency/throughput percentiles and token-cost projections, (4) emits provenance metadata (input probes, seed hash, aggregation weights) and stability scores, and (5) writes composite profiles into the registry. The pipeline runs as a staged job in onboard-worker with safe idempotency keys. The changelog previewer computes deltas (absolute and relative), highlights potential regressions for the top-3 customer workload buckets, and produces a human-readable preview snippet saved alongside the profile draft.

Backward compatibility and opt-out: composition is opt-in via the onboard manifest field "compose_profiles: true". If absent, onboarding preserves current behavior. We added a global feature flag (registry config) to enable automatic composition for accounts that want it.

Testing and validation: unit tests for the aggregator, property tests to ensure deterministic results for identical inputs, integration tests that run small synthetic workloads through the full ingest->compose->publish flow, and an e2e smoke test that exercises changelog preview generation. CI initially exposed a flaky fixture in the ranking comparator; tests were adjusted to be time-insensitive and the CI run was re-triggered.

Rollout and migration notes: composite profiles are created as new variant objects (suffix "-composed"), not by mutating existing variants. This enables safe rollout and easy rollback. A follow-up task is tracked in ENG-11789 to add an automatic canary that will route 10% of traffic to the composed variant for a week before full promotion.

Related work: builds on earlier probe ingestion work (ENG-11234) and complements the release note automation pipeline.

Summary: adds workload-conditioned composition, deterministic provenance sealing, changelog preview generation, feature flag, tests, and docs for operators.

source.title: Add onboard-priority heuristics and contextual changelog extractor
source.author: Maria Chen
source.release_notes: Introduce a priority scoring pipeline for model onboarding to surface high-impact models and a contextual changelog extractor that synthesizes compact release notes from onboarding metadata and sample diffs. Adds admin UI flags for curation and an opt-in human review step for top-priority models.
source.merge_outcome: merged
source.body:
Context and motivation: As the intake stream grows, reviewers and curators are overwhelmed by raw onboard artifacts. This PR introduces a priority-scoring heuristic and an automated, context-aware changelog extractor to make onboarding reviews and release notes generation actionable and concise. The heuristic combines telemetry (sampled latency/throughput), footprint (size, quantization), legal signals (license, embargo), and delta impact (perf regressions vs baseline) into a single probabilistic priority score. The changelog extractor uses metadata diffs, curated prompt-set examples, and example-based summarization rules to produce one-line and paragraph-level release snippets suitable for downstream release automation. Key changes: 1) New pipeline: services/onboard/priority_scorer.py (probabilistic ranker, supports pluggable feature transforms) 2) Migration: db/migrations/20251103_add_priority_scores.sql (adds model_priority_score float, priority_reason text) 3) Changelog extractor: services/onboard/contextual_changelog.py (rules, sample extraction, redact hooks) 4) CLI: tools/onboard-cli add `rank-models` and `preview-changelog` commands to allow fast local curation 5) Admin UI hooks: ui/registry/priority_flag.tsx (opt-in curation pane) 6) Tests and fixtures: tests/onboard/test_priority_scorer.py and tests/onboard/test_changelog_extractor.py 7) Docs: docs/onboard/prioritization.md and docs/onboard/changelog-format.md Implementation notes and tradeoffs:
- Score is probabilistic and intentionally conservative: we apply a sigmoid-scaled ensemble of features so scores stay interpretable across workload changes.
- To avoid false positives for legacy uploads we included a migration TTL and an opt-out metadata tag (metadata.priority_opt_out).
- Changelog extractor emits three formats: short blurb, detailed paragraph, and a compact semantic diff used by release automation.
- Redaction hooks were added to remove PII and large prompt artifacts; these can be extended by enterprise rules.
Checklist:
- [x] Priority scorer implementation
- [x] DB migration and rollback script
- [x] Changelog extractor rules + redaction
- [x] CLI end-to-end commands
- [x] Unit tests for core scoring transforms
- [x] Integration test (local) that runs rank->preview-changelog flow
- [x] Docs and changelog templates
- [x] Admin UI minimal controls
Testing and CI: Initial CI run revealed a flaky test in tests/onboard/test_changelog_extractor.py (timing-sensitive sample replay). I quarantined the test by stabilizing sample fixtures and adding a deterministic RNG seed; rerun passed. CI matrix: linux/py39 unit, integration (local sqlite), linters. Commits summary: [c1f9d2] introduce priority scorer and feature transforms [d2b7e1] add db migration and migration test [e3a4bf] implement contextual changelog extractor and redact hooks [f4b5c6] CLI commands and docs [a7c8dd] admin UI curation pane and wiring [b9e0f1] tests + flakiness fixes High-level files changed: services/onboard/priority_scorer.py services/onboard/contextual_changelog.py tools/onboard_cli.py db/migrations/20251103_add_priority_scores.sql ui/registry/priority_flag.tsx docs/onboard/prioritization.md tests/onboard/test_priority_scorer.py tests/onboard/test_changelog_extractor.py src/registry/serializers.py (small adapter), README additions Review conversation snapshot: "Priya Natarajan: Nice work, this will help triage. Two asks: (1) add a short note in the docs about how scores should be interpreted by SREs; (2) include an integration test that runs the migration and a rollback." "Maria Chen: Thanks—added SRE doc section and a migration rollback test in the latest commit. The rollback test uses a test-only fixture and cleans up inserted rows." "Evan Thompson: Can the scorer be extended to accept per-customer weighting? We should avoid global thresholds where customers' usage differs." "Maria Chen: The scorer accepts a 'weight_map' optional param; CLI passes a default weight_map but admin UI will allow overriding on a per-namespace basis in follow-up (ENG-1234)." "Liam O'Connor: One nit on the changelog extractor: it currently uses a max token cutoff of 512 for sample aggregation—might hide larger examples. Please add a link to stored sample artifacts instead of trimming." "Maria Chen: Updated extractor to emit a storage link when sample truncated; the link uses the same signed-URL mechanism as telemetry snapshots." "Priya Natarajan: Approving pending CI green." Approvals: "Priya Natarajan: approved" "Evan Thompson: approved" CI timeline: "2025-11-04: CI run failed (flaky sample replay)." "2025-11-05: Quarantine commit pushed, CI passed." Merge notes: Squash-merged on 2025-11-18 by Maria Chen; commit message includes ENG-1229 and a concise summary for release notes. Rollout plan:
- Step 1: Deploy scorer service to staging with migration in read-only mode for 48h
- Step 2: Run background scoring batch, validate distribution with curated sample set
- Step 3: Enable admin UI curation for selected reviewers
- Step 4: Unblock migration to write mode and enable changelog autosynth for low-priority models only
- Step 5: Monitor for false positives and adjust weight_map via admin flags
Observability and metrics added:
- onboard.priority.score_histogram
- onboard.changelog.extract_time_ms
- onboard.migration.rows_migrated_count
Known limitations and follow-ups:
- Need per-org default weight profiles (ENG-1234)
- More robust PII redaction for edge-case prompt formats (SEC-501)
- Perf optimization: scorer currently calculates a full feature vector for every model; we will add cached lightweight prefilters in a follow-up (perf ticket ENG-1270)
commits: introduce priority scorer and feature transforms
add db migration and migration test
implement contextual changelog extractor and redact hooks
CLI commands and docs
admin UI curation pane and wiring
tests + flakiness fixes
review_comments: Priya: Please add tests for legacy metadata migration and document the opt-out tag behavior.
Evan: Can we accept a per-customer weight_map? Otherwise thresholds will be misleading for small customers.
Liam: Changelog extractor truncates large samples — add storage link instead of silent truncate.
Maria: Addressed in commits; added migration rollback test and storage links.
source.notes: Edge-case: during staged rollout we disabled autosynth for models with license=proprietary by default to avoid leaking vendor notices. The admin UI exposes a per-model override.
