source.title: add-transitive-dependency-graph-and-rolling-profile-calibration-hook
source.author: Elena Park
source.release_notes: Introduce a transitive dependency graph for models and a rolling profile calibration hook that periodically re-measures model profiles and emits compact changelog deltas. Improves downstream compatibility tagging and automates small profile adjustments without manual release steps.
source.description:
Motivation: onboarding new model families has become harder to maintain when a single upstream weight change ripples through variants (quantized builds, fused kernels, etc). We need a canonical, machine-friendly representation of transitive model dependencies and an automated calibration job that re-evaluates performance profiles on a small set of canonical prompts to keep profile metadata current and to generate compact delta release notes.

What this PR does: adds a lightweight transitive-dependency graph to model metadata, implements a rolling-profile-calibration hook that runs in a low-cost sandbox, and wires the calibration output into the changelog assembler so we can publish concise, delta-based release notes for profile updates. The calibration job is conservative by design: it flags changes, produces a delta bundle, and only updates a profile stamp when thresholds are met or after human approval (configurable).

Design highlights: 1) metadata: ModelProfile now includes fields: `transitive_dependencies` (list of model ids + relation reason), `compatibility_graph_sha` (checksum for graph snapshot), and `calibration_snapshot` (timestamped probe results). 2) calibration hook: a scheduled service that loads the model variant in an isolated runner, runs a curated small corpus of prompts (opt-in per-model), measures latency/throughput/p99 and a few quality metrics (BLEU/ROUGE surrogate, token perplexity where applicable), then produces a normalized profile delta. 3) changelog integration: deltas are compacted and appended to the existing release assembler; small, safe adjustments may be auto-applied if they pass stability checks and the model owner has enabled "auto-calibrate". 4) safety & audit: every auto-change emits an audit record and a signed delta bundle; the console shows a compact human-readable blurb and the raw delta.

Compatibility & migration: existing profiles without transitive graph fields remain valid; the migration job will backfill `compatibility_graph_sha` for models that have origin metadata available. The calibration hook is opt-in by default for older models; new onboarded models get a default small probe set unless opted out.

Testing and validation: unit tests for graph serialization and checksum verification; end-to-end test harness that runs the calibration flow against a small internal model (ci-calib/test-1) to verify non-destructive behavior; load tests to ensure the sandbox runner respects resource limits and does not affect serving pools.

Operational notes: calibration job runs at low priority in the infra sandbox with default cadence of 72 hours; adjustable per-customer; rollback is supported by restoring previous profile JSON and revoking the `compatibility_graph_sha` update.

Open questions / follow-ups: 1) what gating thresholds for auto-apply are appropriate across model families (ENG-5021 is tracking an experimental policy); 2) UX in console for reviewing auto-applied deltas (docs included in this PR).
commits: initial: add transitive dependency schema and serialization (model_profile/schema.py)
service: rolling calibration worker and sandbox runner (calib/worker.py)
integration: changelog assembler hook + delta serializer (changelog/delta_assembler.py)
migration: backfill script for compatibility_graph_sha (scripts/backfill_graph_sha.py)
tests: unit + e2e harness for calibration flow (tests/calib/*)
docs: add registry docs and console guidance for auto-calibrate (docs/registry/calibration.md)
review_threads:
Ava Chen: Please add explicit unit tests for checksum mismatch behavior.
Elena Park: added tests in tests/calib/test_checksum.py and updated the serialization to include stable ordering.

Miguel Torres: How do we avoid the calibration job pushing noisy token-perplexity changes for models with nondeterministic outputs?
Elena Park: calibration reports include a noise estimate (bootstrap) and we only auto-apply when signal-to-noise ratio > 3. Added comment in worker and a small e2e test to validate the logic.

Priya Nair: console UX — we need a one-click revert for auto-applied changes.
Elena Park: added `revert_delta` endpoint and console row action; documented in docs/registry/calibration.md.
source.testing: Unit and integration coverage added: graph serialization (100% on new module), calibration worker logic (bootstrap noise estimation, threshold gating), backfill script dry-run. E2E harness executed in CI against a canned model, verifying no profile mutation unless thresholds met. Human-in-loop approval flow tested in staging via feature flag.
source.perf_summary: Calibration probes use a tiny sample (default 20 prompts) and run in sandbox with CPU/GPU time capped. Observed median calibration job runtime ~38s in staging; added batching to reduce overhead. Typical profile deltas are small (<2% latency change) and compressed delta bundles average 1.6KB.
source.notes_on_migration: Backfill script (scripts/backfill_graph_sha.py) should be run once after merge for orgs that rely on compatibility graph checks. Older models remain compatible; to enable auto-calibrate on an existing model, set metadata flag `auto_calibrate=true` and ensure a probe set is attached.

title: add-transitive-dependency-graph-and-rolling-profile-calibration-hook
preview:
Motivation: onboarding new model families has become harder to maintain when a single upstream weight change ripples through variants (quantized builds, fused kernels, etc). We need a canonical, machine-friendly representation of transitive model dependencies and an automated calibration job that re-evaluates performance profiles on a small set of canonical prompts to keep profile metadata current and to generate compact delta release notes.

What this PR does: adds a lightweight transitive-dependency graph to model metadata, implements a rolling-profile-calibration hook that runs in a low-cost sandbox, and wires the calibration output into the changelog assembler so we can publish concise, delta-based release notes for profile updates. The calibration job is conservative by design: it flags changes, produces a delta bundle, and only updates a profile stamp when thresholds are met or after human approval (configurable).

Design highlights: 1) metadata: ModelProfile now includes fields: `transitive_dependencies` (list of model ids + relation reason), `compatibility_graph_sha` (checksum for graph snapshot), and `calibration_snapshot` (timestamped probe results). 2) calibration hook: a scheduled service that loads the model variant in an isolated runner, runs a curated small corpus of prompts (opt-in per-model), measures latency/throughput/p99 and a few quality metrics (BLEU/ROUGE surrogate, token perplexity where applicable), then produces a normalized profile delta. 3) changelog integration: deltas are compacted and appended to the existing release assembler; small, safe adjustments may be auto-applied if they pass stability checks and the model owner has enabled "auto-calibrate". 4) safety & audit: every auto-change emits an audit record and a signed delta bundle; the console shows a compact human-readable blurb and the raw delta.

Compatibility & migration: existing profiles without transitive graph fields remain valid; the migration job will backfill `compatibility_graph_sha` for models that have origin metadata available. The calibration hook is opt-in by default for older models; new onboarded models get a default small probe set unless opted out.

Testing and validation: unit tests for graph serialization and checksum verification; end-to-end test harness that runs the calibration flow against a small internal model (ci-calib/test-1) to verify non-destructive behavior; load tests to ensure the sandbox runner respects resource limits and does not affect serving pools.

Operational notes: calibration job runs at low priority in the infra sandbox with default cadence of 72 hours; adjustable per-customer; rollback is supported by restoring previous profile JSON and revoking the `compatibility_graph_sha` update.

Open questions / follow-ups: 1) what gating thresholds for auto-apply are appropriate across model families (ENG-5021 is tracking an experimental policy); 2) UX in console for reviewing auto-applied deltas (docs included in this PR).

source.title: Add rw/qwen2.5-72b-instruct to hosted catalog (profiles + provenance)
source.author: Dev Patel
source.description:
Adds a new Hosted API catalog model entry: `rw/qwen2.5-72b-instruct` with default quant profile selection and explicit provenance hashes.

Context
- Requested via ENG-4219 (Solutions: enterprise customer wants Qwen2.5 family parity)
- Applied ML eval gates: all suites passed (links below)
- Runtime perf bar: passed on H100 and L40S with `int8-weight-only` as default
- Security risk review: approved-with-restrictions (see link)

Checklist
- [x] Model ID + alias naming follows standard
- [x] Tokenizer identifier recorded
- [x] Context length limits set (2k/8k/16k)
- [x] Quant profiles added: fp16, int8-weight-only, int4-weight-only (int4 marked N/A on L40S)
- [x] Fallback policy set (fallback to rw/qwen2.5-72b-instruct-fp16 for first 7 days)
- [x] Provenance: sha256 hashes recorded for weights + tokenizer
- [x] Docs note filed (DEVEX-1182)

Links (launch packet)
- Eval harness Suite A/B/C: https://confluence.redwood.local/display/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-run-2025-02-04-qwen2.5-72b
- Tool calling suite: https://confluence.redwood.local/display/applied-ml-and-evals/eval-run-2025-02-04-qwen2.5-72b-tools
- Perf suite run IDs: perf-2025-02-05-1123 (H100), perf-2025-02-05-1188 (L40S)
- Security risk review: https://confluence.redwood.local/display/security-and-compliance/risk-review-qwen2.5-72b
- Rollout plan: https://confluence.redwood.local/display/eng-platform/hosted-model-rollout-and-fallback

Implementation notes
- Added registry entries under `models/hosted/rw-qwen2.5-72b-instruct.yaml` and profiles in `profiles/qwen2.5-72b/`
- Default profile set to `int8-weight-only`
- Added router policy stub for dark launch (disabled by default)

Commits
- registry: add model yaml + aliases
- profiles: add fp16/int8/int4 metadata
- router-policy: add disabled rule

Review conversation (summary)
- Ethan: requested perf table in PR description + clarified L40S int4 status; addressed.
- Olga: asked to include restriction text in docs task; added DEVEX-1182 link + summary line.
- Connor: requested fallback order include cross-family safe default; updated router policy comment.

CI
- lint: pass
- unit: pass
- schema-validate: pass

Merge
- Squash merged to main.
source.release_notes: Hosted catalog: add rw/qwen2.5-72b-instruct (default int8-weight-only), dark launch routing rule created.

source.title: Add v1 quantization profile schema, sample profiles, and registry-side validation
source.author: Quentin Harris
source.description:
This PR introduces the v1 Quantization Profile schema to the model registry, adds example profile JSONs for a small set of initial model variants, and wires validation into the registry publish/update path. The goal is to ensure profiles are versionable, auditable, and safe to consume by downstream control plane/runtime code by enforcing strict constraints at the point of publication.

Summary of changes: 1) Add `quantization_profile.schema.json` (v1) and a typed validator wrapper used by the profile ingestion codepath. 2) Add sample profiles under `examples/quantization_profiles/v1/` to serve as canonical references for Applied ML authors and for CI fixtures. 3) Enforce validation rules in `RegistryProfileService.publishQuantProfile()` and `RegistryProfileService.updateQuantProfile()` with clear error messages intended to surface in API responses and internal tooling. 4) Add unit tests for schema validation, error messaging, and backward-compatible behavior when no quantization profile fields are present.

Key v1 schema fields (high-level): `profile_id` (stable), `profile_version` (semver), `model_id`, `model_variant`, `quant_method` (e.g. awq/gptq/fp8/int8), `weight_precision`, `activation_precision`, `calibration` (dataset ref + hash), `constraints` (gpu_skus, min_driver, max_context_tokens, supported_sequence_length_buckets), `artifacts` (logical names + checksums; actual blob storage and signing will land in follow-up), and `known_caveats` (explicit list for docs/console warnings).

Validation notes: we intentionally validate for determinism and safety: required fields must be present; enums are strict; semver parsing required; `model_id`/`model_variant` must correspond to an existing registry entry; `constraints.max_context_tokens` must be <= model variant max; `gpu_skus` must be from the curated SKU allowlist; and `calibration.dataset_hash` must be a SHA256 hex string. Artifact references are allowed but not required in this PR (to avoid blocking early schema adoption); if present, checksums must be SHA256.

Out of scope: artifact storage/signing (tracked separately), compatibility matrix generation, control-plane route configuration, and runtime application of the profiles.

Why now: downstream teams (Platform + Runtime) are starting integration work and need a stable schema with enforced validation so they can rely on registry reads without defensive parsing in every service. Having canonical examples also reduces churn in field naming and enables docs + SDK examples to point to a consistent format.

Risk: low. Changes are additive and gated to the new profile endpoints; no changes to existing model metadata schema. Existing model publish flows are unaffected unless a quantization profile payload is provided.

Rollout plan: land schema + validator behind existing registry feature gate `registry.quant_profiles.enabled` (default off in prod); use examples + CI tests in this PR; enable gate for internal tooling in staging once follow-up control-plane work is ready.
commits: Add quantization_profile.schema.json (v1) and validator wrapper
Add example v1 quantization profiles for llama-3.1-8b-instruct and qwen2.5-7b-instruct
Wire schema validation into publish/update endpoints and improve error messages
Add unit tests and CI fixtures for profile validation + model/variant checks
Docs: internal README on authoring profiles and running validator locally
review_conversation: Arjun Desai: Can we confirm naming: `profile_id` vs `profile_ref`? I want the on-disk schema to be stable even if the control plane uses refs/aliases later.

Quentin Harris (author): Yep. This PR standardizes on `profile_id` + `profile_version` in the schema. Control plane can reference `profile_id@version` or an alias, but registry canonical identity stays explicit. I added validator rules + comments to discourage implicit 'latest'.
Ethan Park: Please ensure error messages are actionable (Console will likely surface them). Right now the enum error just says 'invalid'. Can we include the allowed values?
Quentin Harris (author): Updated validator to include allowed enum values and, for GPU SKU errors, point to the allowlist constant. Added tests asserting the message includes the allowed values.
Jonas Weber: Example profiles: can we add a note that these are not 'blessed' for quality, just schema examples? Also `supported_sequence_length_buckets` should include 4096 for the models we target.
Quentin Harris (author): Added README note and updated example buckets to include 4096 where applicable. Also added a test that buckets must be strictly increasing and <= `max_context_tokens`.

Marcus Lin: Any security concerns? Please avoid allowing arbitrary URI schemes for calibration/artifacts; we should constrain now to avoid later SSRF-ish patterns if someone tries to use these strings in fetchers.

Quentin Harris (author): Good call. Validator now constrains `dataset_ref` to `redwood://` only for v1. Artifact URIs are logical names only in this PR; actual blob access will be through registry-managed storage in the follow-up signing PR.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash into main
source.release_notes: Internal: model registry now supports v1 quantization profile schema validation and ships canonical example profile JSONs. No customer-facing behavior change.

source.title: Versioned quantization profile artifact storage + signing/integrity verification
source.author: Quentin Harris
source.description:
This PR adds versioned artifact storage for Quantization Profile Library profiles and enforces integrity checks (sha256 + signature verification) on fetch. Motivation: profiles are referenced from route config and must be auditable/reproducible; we need deterministic resolution to an immutable artifact, with a clear deprecation/supersedence story and guardrails against tampering/corruption in object storage.

What’s included:
- Versioned profile artifacts: profile versions now resolve to an immutable `artifact_ref` that points to a content-addressed blob plus a stable registry path.
- Manifest + checksums: each artifact upload produces a manifest with sha256 digests for each payload file and the overall bundle.
- Signing: registry signs the manifest (ed25519) using a KMS-backed key; clients verify signatures before accepting the artifact.
- Integrity verification on read path: registry service verifies sha256 + signature before serving download URLs or returning inline metadata (fail-closed).
- Deprecation fields: adds `deprecated_at`, `deprecation_reason`, and `superseded_by` to profile metadata; publish tooling rejects new references to deprecated versions (unless explicitly forced for rollback/testing).
- Backwards compatibility: existing non-versioned artifacts are supported behind a compatibility shim (read-only); any new publishes require versioned + signed artifacts.

Storage layout:
- Object store key format: `quant-profiles/{model_id}/{profile_id}/v{version}/bundle.tar.zst`
- Manifest stored alongside: `.../manifest.json` and `.../manifest.sig`
- Registry DB stores: `artifact_ref`, `sha256`, `signature`, `signing_key_id`, `created_by`, timestamps.

Threat model / guardrails:
- Prevent accidental corruption: sha256 verification on fetch.
- Prevent unauthorized mutation: signature verification + object store path immutability (write-once semantics at the app layer).
- Key rotation supported via `signing_key_id`; verification trusts a configured allowlist of keys.

Notes:
- This does not change profile schema fields related to quant parameters; it only affects artifact lifecycle and verification.
- The upload/sign path is only enabled for internal publisher tooling in this PR. Public/customer APIs remain read-only.

Testing:
- Unit: manifest generation + signature verification, key allowlist behavior, deprecation gating.
- Integration: upload -> sign -> store -> fetch -> verify -> serve URL.
- Negative tests: tampered manifest, tampered bundle, unknown key id, missing signature.

Rollout:
- Deployed behind `REGISTRY_ENABLE_SIGNED_PROFILE_ARTIFACTS` (default off in prod until publisher tooling is updated).
- Once enabled, new publishes will require signing, but existing profiles continue to resolve via legacy path.

Follow-ups:
- Wire policy engine/runtime consumers to record `artifact_ref` in traces/logs for end-to-end auditability.
- Add registry metrics for verification failures and legacy-path reads.
commits: registry/artifacts: introduce ArtifactRef + versioned storage model
publisher: generate manifest.json w/ per-file sha256 + bundle sha256
security: add ed25519 signing via KMS-backed key + allowlisted verifier
api: include artifact_ref + signing_key_id in profile read responses
deprecation: add deprecated_at / deprecation_reason / superseded_by fields + validation
tests: integration tests for tamper detection + key rotation behavior
docs: internal README for publishing + migration notes
ci_checks: integration tests: pass (rerun 1x after transient object-store timeout)
discussion:
Noah Patel (review): Can we guarantee determinism if the same version is re-published? We need immutability.
Quentin Harris (author): Yes—publisher refuses to overwrite `quant-profiles/{...}/v{version}`. Also `artifact_ref` is content-addressed; registry enforces write-once at the DB layer (unique constraint on (profile_id, version)).

Marcus Lin (review): Please document key rotation + what happens if a key is removed from allowlist.
Quentin Harris (author): Added `signing_key_id` allowlist docs and a runbook note: removing a key will cause verification failures for artifacts signed by that key; intended only for emergency response. We can add new key ids without impacting existing artifacts.

Arjun Desai (review): Deprecation fields—do we block fetch entirely for deprecated profiles? We still need rollback.
Quentin Harris (author): Fetch is allowed; only *publishing* new route references should be blocked by policy/control plane. In registry we mark deprecated and expose it in read APIs; consumers can choose to warn. Added `superseded_by` to guide automated tooling.

Ethan Park (review): Any caching concerns? We will fetch manifests frequently at high QPS.
Quentin Harris (author): Registry caches manifest verification results in-memory keyed by artifact_ref for 10m. Download URLs are minted post-verification. Also added metrics for cache hit/miss to help tune.

Jonas Weber (review): Please add a negative test for partially-corrupt bundles (single file mismatch).
Quentin Harris (author): Added `tamper_single_file` case that modifies one entry inside the tarball and asserts sha mismatch triggers 400 + `PROFILE_ARTIFACT_INTEGRITY_FAILED`.

- Integrity verification on read path: registry service verifies sha256 + signature before serving download URLs or returning inline metadata (fail-closed).
- Deprecation fields: adds `deprecated_at`, `deprecation_reason`, and `superseded_by` to profile metadata; publish tooling rejects new references to deprecated versions (unless explicitly forced for rollback/testing).
- Backwards compatibility: existing non-versioned artifacts are supported behind a compatibility shim (read-only); any new publishes require versioned + signed artifacts.

Storage layout:
- Object store key format: `quant-profiles/{model_id}/{profile_id}/v{version}/bundle.tar.zst`
- Manifest stored alongside: `.../manifest.json` and `.../manifest.sig`
- Registry DB stores: `artifact_ref`, `sha256`, `signature`, `signing_key_id`, `created_by`, timestamps.

Threat model / guardrails:
- Prevent accidental corruption: sha256 verification on fetch.
- Prevent unauthorized mutation: signature verification + object store path immutability (write-once semantics at the app layer).
- Key rotation supported via `signing_key_id`; verification trusts a configured allowlist of keys.

Notes:
- This does not change profile schema fields related to quant parameters; it only affects artifact lifecycle and verification.
- The upload/sign path is only enabled for internal publisher tooling in this PR. Public/customer APIs remain read-only.

Testing:
- Unit: manifest generation + signature verification, key allowlist behavior, deprecation gating.
- Integration: upload -> sign -> store -> fetch -> verify -> serve URL.
- Negative tests: tampered manifest, tampered bundle, unknown key id, missing signature.

Rollout:
- Deployed behind `REGISTRY_ENABLE_SIGNED_PROFILE_ARTIFACTS` (default off in prod until publisher tooling is updated).
- Once enabled, new publishes will require signing, but existing profiles continue to resolve via legacy path.

Follow-ups:
- Wire policy engine/runtime consumers to record `artifact_ref` in traces/logs for end-to-end auditability.
- Add registry metrics for verification failures and legacy-path reads.
text:
This PR adds versioned artifact storage for Quantization Profile Library profiles and enforces integrity checks (sha256 + signature verification) on fetch. Motivation: profiles are referenced from route config and must be auditable/reproducible; we need deterministic resolution to an immutable artifact, with a clear deprecation/supersedence story and guardrails against tampering/corruption in object storage.

What’s included:
- Versioned profile artifacts: profile versions now resolve to an immutable `artifact_ref` that points to a content-addressed blob plus a stable registry path.
- Manifest + checksums: each artifact upload produces a manifest with sha256 digests for each payload file and the overall bundle.
- Signing: registry signs the manifest (ed25519) using a KMS-backed key; clients verify signatures before accepting the artifact.
- Integrity verification on read path: registry service verifies sha256 + signature before serving download URLs or returning inline metadata (fail-closed).
- Deprecation fields: adds `deprecated_at`, `deprecation_reason`, and `superseded_by` to profile metadata; publish tooling rejects new references to deprecated versions (unless explicitly forced for rollback/testing).
- Backwards compatibility: existing non-versioned artifacts are supported behind a compatibility shim (read-only); any new publishes require versioned + signed artifacts.

Storage layout:
- Object store key format: `quant-profiles/{model_id}/{profile_id}/v{version}/bundle.tar.zst`
- Manifest stored alongside: `.../manifest.json` and `.../manifest.sig`
- Registry DB stores: `artifact_ref`, `sha256`, `signature`, `signing_key_id`, `created_by`, timestamps.

Threat model / guardrails:
- Prevent accidental corruption: sha256 verification on fetch.
- Prevent unauthorized mutation: signature verification + object store path immutability (write-once semantics at the app layer).
- Key rotation supported via `signing_key_id`; verification trusts a configured allowlist of keys.

Notes:
- This does not change profile schema fields related to quant parameters; it only affects artifact lifecycle and verification.
- The upload/sign path is only enabled for internal publisher tooling in this PR. Public/customer APIs remain read-only.

Testing:
- Unit: manifest generation + signature verification, key allowlist behavior, deprecation gating.
- Integration: upload -> sign -> store -> fetch -> verify -> serve URL.
- Negative tests: tampered manifest, tampered bundle, unknown key id, missing signature.

Rollout:
- Deployed behind `REGISTRY_ENABLE_SIGNED_PROFILE_ARTIFACTS` (default off in prod until publisher tooling is updated).
- Once enabled, new publishes will require signing, but existing profiles continue to resolve via legacy path.

Follow-ups:
- Wire policy engine/runtime consumers to record `artifact_ref` in traces/logs for end-to-end auditability.
- Add registry metrics for verification failures and legacy-path reads.

source.title: add runtime-affinity prober and changelog-hint exporter
source.author: Maya Patel
source.release_notes: Introduce a lightweight runtime-affinity probe that collects short, representative traces for a model variant and emits an annotated performance mapping plus suggested changelog hints. Includes onboarding doc and playback test harness.
source.description:
Motivation and summary:
This PR introduces a lightweight 'runtime-affinity prober' (affinity_prober) that runs short, deterministic probes across a small battery of inference shapes (seq lengths, batch sizes, kv-cache warm/cold) and collates an annotated performance map for model variants. The primary goal is to supply the model registry with structured, human-readable hints for release notes (e.g., 'suitable for 8k tokens with quantized-llm on A100; expected median p95 latency 42ms at batch=1') and to provide downstream deploy tooling with an initial affinity signal for placement and scheduling decisions.

What this changes (high level):
- registry/probes/affinity_prober.py: new probe runner with pluggable backends (mock backend for tests + runtime adapter).
- registry/annotators/perf_hint_exporter.py: turns probe output into compact annotations and suggested one-line changelog snippets.
- onboarding/docs/perf-probe.md: walkthrough for maintainers (how to run locally and interpret results).
- tests/test_affinity_prober.py and tests/test_perf_hint_exporter.py: unit + integration mocks.
- registry/schema/affinity_annotation_v1.yaml: small schema for annotations.
- examples/onboard/sample-probe-config.yaml: sample config used by CI for smoke runs.

Description / checklist:
- [x] Implement probe runner core + backend adapter pattern
- [x] Add exporter that synthesizes short release hints and a structured annotation payload
- [x] Add schema and validation for annotation bundle
- [x] Unit tests for logic paths and CI smoke config
- [x] Documentation: onboarding doc + example config
- [x] Integration test that runs against registry's mock-runtime in CI (short, <30s)

Commits (summary):
- c1a2b3: initial affinity prober interface + mock backend
- d4e5f6: add perf hint exporter and annotation schema; wire to onboard pipeline
- a7b8c9: tests and docs; CI smoke runner and sample configs

Changed files (high level snippets):
- registry/probes/affinity_prober.py (new)
  - core class: AffinityProber(collections.namedtuple) -> run_probe() returns AnnotationBundle
  - supports probes: short_seq, long_seq, kv_warm, batch_pathology
- registry/annotators/perf_hint_exporter.py (new)
  - function: generate_release_hints(annotation) -> {blurb, tags, perf_buckets}
- registry/schema/affinity_annotation_v1.yaml (new)
source.description_continued:
Review conversation (high level):
- Priya Natarajan (reviewer): asked for clearer separation between probe sampling and billing-sensitive telemetry. Requested: ensure the probe runner defaults to local-only and never emits customer telemetry without explicit opt-in.
  - Author reply (Maya): added an opt-in flag and documented the privacy boundary in onboarding doc.
- Samir Gupta (reviewer): requested deterministic seeding for probes and a fast-fail path when runtime backend is unavailable.
  - Author reply: added deterministic seeding and an adapter timeout with a clear error type; added unit tests for timeout behavior.
- Alejandro Ruiz (reviewer): suggested expanding the exporter to include a 'confidence' field and to format the hint to be copy-paste friendly for changelogs.
  - Author reply: added confidence calculation (sample variance heuristic) and improved exporter templates; included examples in docs.

CI and validation notes:
- CI matrix: lint (flake8 / mypy), unit tests, registration schema validation, short prow perf smoke (mock-runtime).
- First CI run: lint passed, unit tests failed (flake: test timeout on mock backend).
- Fixes: increased mock backend timeout and added deterministic seed; re-run CI -> all checks passed.
- Performance smoke: synthetic run estimates a median p95 that aligns with the values emitted by the exporter; note in CI logs flagged a test as slow once (flaky), retried -> passed.

Backward compatibility and safety:
- The annotation schema is additive and uses a v1 namespace (affinity_annotation_v1); existing consumers will ignore unknown fields.
- The exporter is additive: it writes optional annotation bundles to the registry but does not change existing onboarding flows unless the 'apply-hints' flag is enabled by maintainers.

Manual testing performed:
- Ran affinity_prober against the local mock-runtime and a narrow variant of a quantized LLM to validate kv-cache warm vs cold behavior.
- Verified exporter templates produce 1-2 line changelog suggestions and a JSON annotation that rounds to expected buckets.
- Docs walkthrough executed by a reviewer (Priya) following onboarding doc.

Notes for release engineers / next steps:
- Consider wiring the exporter output into the existing 'autodraft' release generator as an optional source of truth (ENG-8999 is tracking).
- We should add a longer-running nightly probe job to collect stability footprints across real infra; this PR keeps the prober lightweight by design.

Merge outcome:
- Approved by reviewers: Priya Natarajan, Samir Gupta, Alejandro Ruiz.
- CI: passing after one rerun.
- Merge: squash, commit message aggregates the three commits and documents the schema.

If you'd like to run this locally: follow onboarding/docs/perf-probe.md and use examples/onboard/sample-probe-config.yaml.

Files changed (explicit list):
- registry/probes/affinity_prober.py
- registry/annotators/perf_hint_exporter.py
- registry/schema/affinity_annotation_v1.yaml
- onboarding/docs/perf-probe.md
- examples/onboard/sample-probe-config.yaml
- tests/test_affinity_prober.py
- tests/test_perf_hint_exporter.py
- .github/workflows/probe-smoke.yml (smallness guard for CI)

---
Disclaimer: this PR purposefully focuses on generating conservative, opt-in signals for release notes and placement decisions. It does not attempt to replace end-to-end performance benchmarking or sanctioned SLA reporting.
commit_summary: initial affinity prober interface + mock backend
add perf hint exporter and annotation schema; wire to onboard pipeline
tests, docs, and CI smoke runner
human_readable_changes: Adds onboarding docs and example configs
Adds unit and integration smoke tests

source.title: policy-aware-onboard-reviewer-bot-and-compact-annotation-generator
source.author: Maya Chen
source.release_notes: Add a policy-aware reviewer bot that annotates model onboarding PRs with human-readable policy findings, auto-suggests rollback manifests for flagged models, and generates compact release note snippets for changelogs.
source.description:
Context and motivation: Onboarding new model versions frequently requires cross-checking provenance, licenses, performance baselines, and safety policy assertions. Review cycles are slowed by repetitive checklist items and by non-standardized annotation formats in PRs. This change introduces a lightweight 'onboard-reviewer-bot' service and a compact annotation schema to accelerate reviews, surface policy-level risks in human-readable language, and produce suggested rollback manifests and changelog snippets when onboarding fails checks.

Primary goals: 1) Run deterministic policy checks (license, KMS usage, dataset license tags) and lightweight performance delta checks against stored baselines. 2) Present concise, human-readable annotations in the PR description so reviewers can make decisions quickly. 3) When a critical check fails, create a suggested rollback manifest (k8s manifest fragment) and a one-line changelog blurb for release automation. 4) Fit within existing model-registry hooks and be configurable via repo-level .onboardrc.

What changed (high level): - New service: tools/onboard-bot/ (Go) with a small HTTP webhook receiver and a lightweight policy engine that runs a combination of JSON-schema linting and rule-based checks. - New validators: registry/validators/policy_checks.py (Python) to run in CI for cross-language parity. - Annotation schema: docs/onboard-annotations.md describing the compact, human-readable annotation format and severity tiers. - Rollback generator: tools/onboard-bot/rollback_generator.go generates a k8s-style manifest fragment when a critical policy or performance regression is detected. - Integration: .github/workflows/onboard-bot.yml to run bot checks for PRs labeled 'onboard'. - Tests: tests/onboard-bot/* unit tests + integration harness using a recorded fixture of model metadata.

Commits (summary): - 8f4b2c0: initial crate for onboard-bot service and HTTP webhook receiver - 1a7d3f1: add policy rule engine (license, kms, provenance) - b2d8a3e: rollback manifest generator and compact changelog blurber - c3f0d5a: Python parity validators and shared schema definitions - d4a6e70: docs for annotation schema and admin config (.onboardrc) - e1f2b20: CI workflow and test fixtures - f9a0c11: address reviewer feedback, reduce log noise and tighten timeouts

Files changed (high-level): - tools/onboard-bot/main.go (new) - tools/onboard-bot/rollback_generator.go (new) - tools/onboard-bot/policy_engine.go (new) - registry/validators/policy_checks.py (new) - registry/schemas/onboard_schema.json (new) - .github/workflows/onboard-bot.yml (new) - docs/onboard-annotations.md (new) - .onboardrc.example (new) - tests/onboard-bot/test_policy_engine.py (new) - tests/onboard-bot/fixtures/model-v1.json (new) - README.md (updated) - docs/onboarding.md (updated) - scripts/deploy_onboard_bot.sh (new) - tests/ci/integration_onboard.sh (updated)

Small illustrative snippet (annotation format example): "annotation:severity=warning;check=performance-diff;message='Throughput -18% vs baseline (p99 stable) — investigate batching config.'"

Review conversation (chronological): - Ethan Park (requested changes, 2025-01-16): "This is promising work — two asks before merge: 1) make sure the policy rules are configurable per-namespace via .onboardrc; 2) the rollback manifest needs a dry-run flag so we don't accidentally apply anything during automation. Also please add unit tests for the KMS rule."

- Maya Chen (author reply, 2025-01-16): "Thanks — added .onboardrc support and a dry-run toggle to rollback_generator.go, and tests for KMS rule in tests/onboard-bot. PTAL."

- Leah Morales (comment, 2025-01-17): "Docs look good; can we add a short section showing how the compact changelog blurb maps to release note templates? Also, consider a sample optical layout for annotations in the PR body so it is scannable."

- Maya Chen (update, 2025-01-17): "Added docs/onboard-annotations.md with mapping examples and a recommended PR body layout. Reduced annotation verbosity by collapsing non-critical findings into a single summary line."

- Diego Torres (approval, 2025-01-20): "Approved after CI stabilized. One nit: consider adding a telemetry counter for 'annotation_count' so we can monitor annotation churn over time."

- Maya Chen (follow-up, 2025-01-20): "Added basic telemetry counters (increment-only) and a TODO to expand labels in future PRs."

CI summary: - First run (2025-01-16): lint passed, unit tests: fail (flaky fixture for KMS in CI container), integration tests: pending. - Second run after fix (2025-01-17): lint passed, unit tests: pass, integration smoke: fail (timeout on container startup). - Reran with increased timeouts and fixture pin (2025-01-20): all checks passed. Final status: pass.

Security and compliance notes: - All license checks are rule-based and map to registry/schemas/onboard_schema.json; failures are high-severity by default. - Rollback manifests are output as suggestion files in tools/onboard-bot/suggestions/ and never auto-applied; the webhook requires an explicit admin action to promote suggestions. - No secret material is stored by the bot; KMS rules only validate that a key reference exists and matches allowed patterns.

Backward-compatibility: - The annotation format is additive and non-breaking; existing onboarding flows will ignore new annotations when not consumed. - .onboardrc is optional; default organization-level policy applies when missing.

Operational notes and rollout plan: 1) Deploy webhook receiver to staging (k8s helm chart not included in this PR) and enable via .github labels on a small subset of model registry PRs. 2) Observe annotation_count and false-positive rate for two weeks. 3) Iterate on rule thresholds and add more performance checks (latency percentiles) in follow-ups.

Merge outcome: Merged via squash on 2025-01-22 after CI green and approvals from Diego and Leah. Changelog auto-generated snippet stored in registry/onboard-releases/2025-01-22.md for release automation.

Related work: ENG-4781 (policy-driven onboarding), ENG-4990 (changelog automation), SUP-882 (support escalation for KMS edge-cases).
source.notes: This PR intentionally keeps the bot as a minimal binary with clear boundaries; heavy-weight evaluation logic remains in the registry backend for now. Follow-up PR will add p99 latency checks and extend changelog templates.

Files changed (high-level): - tools/onboard-bot/main.go (new) - tools/onboard-bot/rollback_generator.go (new) - tools/onboard-bot/policy_engine.go (new) - registry/validators/policy_checks.py (new) - registry/schemas/onboard_schema.json (new) - .github/workflows/onboard-bot.yml (new) - docs/onboard-annotations.md (new) - .onboardrc.example (new) - tests/onboard-bot/test_policy_engine.py (new) - tests/onboard-bot/fixtures/model-v1.json (new) - README.md (updated) - docs/onboarding.md (updated) - scripts/deploy_onboard_bot.sh (new) - tests/ci/integration_onboard.sh (updated)

Small illustrative snippet (annotation format example): "annotation:severity=warning;check=performance-diff;message='Throughput -18% vs baseline (p99 stable) — investigate batching config.'"

Review conversation (chronological): - Ethan Park (requested changes, 2025-01-16): "This is promising work — two asks before merge: 1) make sure the policy rules are configurable per-namespace via .onboardrc; 2) the rollback manifest needs a dry-run flag so we don't accidentally apply anything during automation. Also please add unit tests for the KMS rule."

- Maya Chen (author reply, 2025-01-16): "Thanks — added .onboardrc support and a dry-run toggle to rollback_generator.go, and tests for KMS rule in tests/onboard-bot. PTAL."

- Leah Morales (comment, 2025-01-17): "Docs look good; can we add a short section showing how the compact changelog blurb maps to release note templates? Also, consider a sample optical layout for annotations in the PR body so it is scannable."

- Maya Chen (update, 2025-01-17): "Added docs/onboard-annotations.md with mapping examples and a recommended PR body layout. Reduced annotation verbosity by collapsing non-critical findings into a single summary line."

- Diego Torres (approval, 2025-01-20): "Approved after CI stabilized. One nit: consider adding a telemetry counter for 'annotation_count' so we can monitor annotation churn over time."

- Maya Chen (follow-up, 2025-01-20): "Added basic telemetry counters (increment-only) and a TODO to expand labels in future PRs."

CI summary: - First run (2025-01-16): lint passed, unit tests: fail (flaky fixture for KMS in CI container), integration tests: pending. - Second run after fix (2025-01-17): lint passed, unit tests: pass, integration smoke: fail (timeout on container startup). - Reran with increased timeouts and fixture pin (2025-01-20): all checks passed. Final status: pass.

Security and compliance notes: - All license checks are rule-based and map to registry/schemas/onboard_schema.json; failures are high-severity by default. - Rollback manifests are output as suggestion files in tools/onboard-bot/suggestions/ and never auto-applied; the webhook requires an explicit admin action to promote suggestions. - No secret material is stored by the bot; KMS rules only validate that a key reference exists and matches allowed patterns.

Backward-compatibility: - The annotation format is additive and non-breaking; existing onboarding flows will ignore new annotations when not consumed. - .onboardrc is optional; default organization-level policy applies when missing.

Operational notes and rollout plan: 1) Deploy webhook receiver to staging (k8s helm chart not included in this PR) and enable via .github labels on a small subset of model registry PRs. 2) Observe annotation_count and false-positive rate for two weeks. 3) Iterate on rule thresholds and add more performance checks (latency percentiles) in follow-ups.

Merge outcome: Merged via squash on 2025-01-22 after CI green and approvals from Diego and Leah. Changelog auto-generated snippet stored in registry/onboard-releases/2025-01-22.md for release automation.

Related work: ENG-4781 (policy-driven onboarding), ENG-4990 (changelog automation), SUP-882 (support escalation for KMS edge-cases).

source.title: Add usage-aware profile stitcher and autobrief generation for onboarding
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: When multiple intake profiles for the same model arrive from different partners/environments we need a deterministic, usage-weighted reconciliation so the registry surface (performance profiles, recommended quantization, changelog blurb) reflects real customer signal rather than noisy probes. This PR introduces a usage-aware 'profile stitcher' that merges incoming onboarding profiles using per-sample weights derived from telemetry and dataset provenance. It also adds an autobrief generator that drafts concise release notes summarizing changes between the active profile and the new stitched profile.

What this PR does (high level):
- Add profile stitcher implementation that: deduplicates metrics, normalizes latency/throughput histograms, applies per-field weighting, and resolves conflicting knobs (quantization/affordances) via configurable heuristics.
- Add autobrief generator that summarizes: top metric deltas, notable config drift (e.g., sequence length, batchability), recommended rollout safety notes, and suggested canary thresholds.
- Add DB migration: profile_stitch_runs table + indexing to support audit and rollback.
- Add CLI and API hooks to invoke stitching as part of onboard pipeline (on-demand and scheduled).
- Add unit/integration tests and small e2e fixture to validate summaries and determinism.
- Update onboarding docs with configuration knobs and migration notes.

Checklist:
- [x] Implementation of stitcher core algorithm and config
- [x] Autobrief text templates + examples
- [x] DB migration and rollback path
- [x] Tests for deterministic output with fixed seed
- [x] Docs and example CLI usage
- [x] Performance microbench harness for stitcher (latency under typical payload sizes)

Impact and safety: The new stitcher is opt-in by default for existing models; the onboard pipeline exposes a flag `--stitch-mode=off|sampled|always`. For safety we default to `sampled` which runs stitcher asynchronously and writes a draft autobrief for manual review before rolling into the active profile. The autobrief is intentionally conservative in recommendations; full automated rollouts require an additional opt-in.

Migration notes: Run the included migration to create `profile_stitch_runs`. Existing profiles are unaffected until a stitch is executed. See docs/onboarding/usage-stitcher.md for CLI examples and the rollback procedure.

Related work: builds on ENG-3180 (profile canonicalizer) and ENG-2872 (release blurb schema).
commits: a1f4b2e: add profile stitcher core, initial tests
3c9d712: autobrief generator and template examples
b7d44f0: db migration: create profile_stitch_runs table
e5a2d11: CLI flags and API hook wiring
f2c8a60: docs and examples; performance microbench
review_comments: Diego Morales: Can we avoid a full histogram recompute for large sample sets? Suggest incremental merge and a capped sample reservoir - addressed in latest commit with reservoir sampler.
Priya Nair: The autobrief language is great but we should avoid numeric thresholds in the first draft; reviewers might misinterpret. Updated templates to include relative qualifiers.
Liam O'Connor: Add an explicit opt-out path for existing CI release gates; otherwise orgs might get unexpected drafts. Added `--stitch-mode=off` and docs note.
source.performance_notes: Microbench results: stitcher processes 10k small profiles (~3k samples each) in 18s wall time (p95 22s) on m5.4xlarge equivalent. Memory bounded by reservoir size; default reservoir 10k keeps peak under 4GB.
source.release_notes: Introduce usage-aware profile stitching and autobriefing. Adds an opt-in onboarding mode that generates concise, reviewer-friendly release drafts and a DB audit table for stitch runs. Improves registry signal quality by weighting real telemetry during profile reconciliation.
source.notes_for_ops: After deploy: run migration, set default stitch mode at org level if desired, and notify platform teams that autobrief drafts will appear in onboarding UI under 'Draft releases'. Monitor `profile_stitch_runs` table growth and prune older entries as described in the ops guide.
source.tests_added: tests/test_profile_stitcher.py; tests/fixtures/sample_profiles.json; integration fixture under tests/integration/onboard_stitcher.yaml
source.backport_plan: No backport planned; feature is gated behind CLI flag. If required, create cherry-pick to release branch and include migration script during maintenance window.
source.notes: This PR intentionally keeps the autobrief conservative; follow-ups will add style variants for marketing vs engineering audiences and a small ML scorer to prioritize issues mentioned in the brief.

source.title: Introduce probe-schema sampler and release-notes weaver
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: onboarding a model currently produces large, noisy changelogs and our sampling for profile calibration is expensive and sometimes biased by a few long sequences. This PR introduces (1) a small, explicit 'probe schema' for captured onboarding probes (tokenizer id, sequence length histogram, canonical prompts, perf anchors) and (2) a sampler implementation that selects representative probe subsets by stratified length buckets + semantic clustering so downstream profiling and release-note diffs are stable and cheap. Additionally this PR adds a release-notes 'weaver' that synthesizes concise human-readable release blurbs from profile diffs and telemetry deltas (latency, token-cost, quality regression hints). The weaver outputs a compact markdown snippet and a machine-readable changelog fragment for the console UI.

What this PR changes (high level): added a probe schema and serializers; a cluster-based sampler; a release-weaver CLI and library; integration with the model-intake pipeline to emit compact changelog fragments; docs and tests. The description below is intentionally a single continuous string used as the PR body in the repo.

Checklist: - [x] Probe schema (JSON Schema + Python dataclass) and serializer/deserializer - [x] Sampler: stratified-length + embedding-cluster sampler with configurable budget - [x] Release-weaver engine: templates + delta heuristics + CLI (rgm release-weave) - [x] Small compatibility shim in intake hook to attach probe-summary to profile bundles - [x] Unit tests + integration workflow - [x] Docs for registry operators and example outputs - [x] Feature-flag gating (registry.feature.release_weaver) for gradual rollout

Why this approach: stratified-length sampling reduces bias from tail examples; embedding-based clustering keeps semantic diversity; explicit probe schema makes diffs deterministic and reduces churn in release notes. The weaver focuses on delta-first blurbs: e.g. "+12ms p95 (seq<=256), -3% token-cost via quantized variant".

Related: ENG-4278 (probe-driven onboarding), ENG-4301 (release automation), earlier telemetry work on profile stability. Links: https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4278, https://linear.app/redwood/issue/ENG-4301
commits: a9f3bf2 add probe schema dataclass and JSON schema
d1c4e81 implement stratified-length sampler and cluster reducer
e73b401 add release-weaver library and CLI, docs, tests
review_conversation: 2025-01-17 Diego Martinez: Nice direction. Can we keep the probe schema minimal? I worry about downstream storage cost if we store full examples inline.
2025-01-17 Ava Chen: Agreed. Schema stores canonical prompt ids and a small exemplar pointer; full examples remain in blobstore. Updated schema to reference blobs instead of embedding full text.
2025-01-18 Priya Nair: Tests look good but one flaky integration test on CI failed (timing). Recommend adding a deterministic seeding path for the sampler so CI is stable.
2025-01-18 Ava Chen: Added deterministic seed override and a small test that verifies cluster selection is stable with a fixed seed.
2025-01-19 Tomás Rojas: Release-weaver heuristics look useful. Request: add a safety line when confidence < 0.6 and include a link to perf dashboard for manual review.
2025-01-19 Ava Chen: Added confidence threshold and link output; feature gated behind registry.feature.release_weaver for initial rollout.
2025-01-20 CI bot: initial test run failed (one unit test). Rerun triggered by author.
2025-01-20 CI bot: rerun passed (all checks green).
2025-01-20 Diego Martinez: Approved and merged (squash).
source.release_notes: Added probe schema and representative sampler to produce stable performance profiles; new release-weaver automates compact changelog snippets from profile diffs. CLI: rgm release-weave; feature gated. Operators: see docs/registry/release_weaver.md for rollout steps and example outputs.
source.notes_for_backport: This change is non-breaking; if backporting to release branches, disable release_weaver feature flag by default. Backport recommended only for customers who opted into automated changelogs.
source.testing_summary: Unit tests for schema and sampler; integration workflow validates end-to-end probe->profile->weave with fixed seed; CI workflow added to run on intake changes. Observed: p95 stability improved on sampled trials (reduction in churn).
source.implementation_impact: Small API additions under src/sampler/* and new CLI. No changes to existing profile storage format; the probe-summary attachment is optional and backward-compatible.
source.changelog_fragment: weaver: synthesize compact release blurbs from profile diffs; sampler: representative probe sampling reduces changelog churn and profiling cost.

source.title: Introduce sidecar metadata augmenter and contextual release clipper
source.author: Priya Varma
source.description: Motivation: Add a lightweight "sidecar" service that augments model profiles at intake time with runtime telemetry hints, provenance snippets and prioritized impact signals, and a companion 'release clipper' that generates minimal, contextual changelog fragments automatically for model version rollouts. This reduces manual churn when onboarding models and helps signal which metadata deltas are likely to affect serving economics or quality. Design overview: (1) augmenter sidecar runs during profile ingestion and attaches: sampled latency histograms, hardware-affinity hints, embedding-dimension provenance, and a small 'culprit' tag computed by delta heuristics; (2) release clipper consumes profile metadata deltas and telemetry to emit a compact one-line changelog entry + 2-line rationale suitable for release notes and downstream consumers. Implementation highlights: new microservice written in Python using async intake, small redis cache for ephemeral affinity lookups, delta scorer library shared with registry core. Backwards compatibility: sidecar augments are additive to existing profile schema; no mandatory fields are removed. Checklist: - implement augmenter service and unit/integration tests; - wire clipper into release pipeline; - add short docs and onboarding guide; - add e2e test that covers a model intake -> augmenter -> clip generation. Related: ENG-1987 for priority sampling; SUP-4321 support ticket requested more granular changelogs. Rollout plan: feature-flag gated rollout for 2 weeks with telemetry on clipped-changelog-usage; opt-out label for customers who require pristine profile blobs. Notes: kept changelog snippets intentionally conservative to avoid noisy releases; scoring heuristics tuned to favor performance-impacting changes (kv-cache-affinity, seq-length-profile shifts, quantization metadata).
commits_summary: init: add augmenter service skeleton and async intake adapter
feat: integrate delta-scorer and culprit-tag heuristics
feat: add release clipper generator and CLI to emit compact blurb
test: add unit tests for scorer + e2e for clip generation
ci: add augmenter pipeline job and linting rules
docs: sidecar onboarding guide and changelog examples
chore: helm chart and deploy script for sidecar
review_comments: Sofia Ruiz: Nice decomposition. Can we make the delta scorer deterministic by default? Randomized sampling makes reproducibility harder for audits.
Priya Varma: Acknowledged; added deterministic mode and a config param SAMPLE_SEED in next patch.
Ethan Park: Two nits: 1) rename `culprit_score` to `impact_score` for clarity, 2) add a short TTL doc for the redis entries.
Priya Varma: Renamed field and added redis TTL note to docs/onboarding/sidecar-augmenter.md.
Maya Chen: CI flake on integration test when run in parallel; looks like port collision in the test harness.
Priya Varma: Adjusted tests to use ephemeral ports and bumped retries; re-ran CI and green.
source.merge_outcome: Merged via squash. Commit message condensed to: 'introduce sidecar augmenter and release clipper: delta scorer, CLI, docs, helm'.
source.release_notes: Add sidecar metadata augmenter to attach runtime telemetry and provenance to model profiles; introduce contextual release clipper to auto-generate concise changelog snippets highlighting high-impact metadata deltas. Feature-flagged rollout; see docs/onboarding/sidecar-augmenter.md for opt-out and config.
source.notes_for_oncall: If autotagged changelogs appear noisy, reduce sampling window via AUGMENTER_SAMPLING_WINDOW_SECONDS or temporarily disable feature flag 'sidecar-augmenter' in the feature flag service. Monitor metric registry.augmenter.clipper.emitted_count for surge.

source.title: Introduce annotated affinity stamps and chunked changelog compressor
source.author: Ava Chen
source.description:
Motivation: onboarding high-capacity and quantized variants has become noisier in release notes and harder to map to hardware guidance for infra. This PR introduces two coordinated pieces: 1) "annotated affinity stamps" — a small, versioned metadata artifact attached to model variants that captures recommended hardware affinity (e.g. ampere-80, triton-quant-fast, int8-friendly), expected memory working set, and recommended quantization knobs; and 2) a "chunked changelog compressor" — a post-processing step that consumes model intake diffs and emits condensed release chunks (human-friendly bullets + linked deep diffs) so changelogs stay readable for large model updates. Goals: reduce support noise during rollouts, surface deterministic hardware affinity suggestions to serving/runtime teams, and keep changelog summaries concise while preserving drill-down details.

What this PR does (high-level): added a new affinity annotation schema and signer for variant manifests; an upload hook that auto-populates affinity hints based on profiling heuristics and historical run-affinity matches; a compact serializer and compressor that groups changelog hunks into prioritized "chunks" and auto-generates teaser lines for release notes; integration tests and docs; migration tool to backfill affinity stamps for recent variants.

Implementation notes: affinity stamps are stored as a small stamped JSON file in the model bundle (affinity-stamp-v1.json) and a lightweight provenance hash is appended to the variant metadata. The chunked changelog compressor runs as a transform in the release pipeline (release-assembler) and uses heuristics: group by semantic change type (perf/regression/config), weight by user-facing impact (example-driven), and produce at most 6 top-level bullets with links to expanded hunks. The compressor is deterministic given the same diff seed and seed version.

Backwards compatibility and rollout: old variants without affinity stamps continue to function — runtime falls back to default routing. The upload hook is opt-in by default; orgs can enable auto-stamping via a feature flag at intake. The migration/backfill job writes affinity stamps as a separate commit and is reversible.

Testing/validation: unit tests for serializer, end-to-end integration with release-assembler in CI, a sampled backfill run capturing 500 variants for validation, and a small canary deploy of the compressed changelog to the docs pipeline.

Doc changes: docs/onboard/affinity-stamps.md, docs/release/changelog-compressor.md and a short console UI blurb to surface the top-3 affinity suggestions in the variant detail page.

Related: refers to ENG-4232 (affinity heuristics prototype) and ENG-4301 (changelog UX revamp).

title: Introduce annotated affinity stamps and chunked changelog compressor
preview:
Motivation: onboarding high-capacity and quantized variants has become noisier in release notes and harder to map to hardware guidance for infra. This PR introduces two coordinated pieces: 1) "annotated affinity stamps" — a small, versioned metadata artifact attached to model variants that captures recommended hardware affinity (e.g. ampere-80, triton-quant-fast, int8-friendly), expected memory working set, and recommended quantization knobs; and 2) a "chunked changelog compressor" — a post-processing step that consumes model intake diffs and emits condensed release chunks (human-friendly bullets + linked deep diffs) so changelogs stay readable for large model updates. Goals: reduce support noise during rollouts, surface deterministic hardware affinity suggestions to serving/runtime teams, and keep changelog summaries concise while preserving drill-down details.

What this PR does (high-level): added a new affinity annotation schema and signer for variant manifests; an upload hook that auto-populates affinity hints based on profiling heuristics and historical run-affinity matches; a compact serializer and compressor that groups changelog hunks into prioritized "chunks" and auto-generates teaser lines for release notes; integration tests and docs; migration tool to backfill affinity stamps for recent variants.

Implementation notes: affinity stamps are stored as a small stamped JSON file in the model bundle (affinity-stamp-v1.json) and a lightweight provenance hash is appended to the variant metadata. The chunked changelog compressor runs as a transform in the release pipeline (release-assembler) and uses heuristics: group by semantic change type (perf/regression/config), weight by user-facing impact (example-driven), and produce at most 6 top-level bullets with links to expanded hunks. The compressor is deterministic given the same diff seed and seed version.

Backwards compatibility and rollout: old variants without affinity stamps continue to function — runtime falls back to default routing. The upload hook is opt-in by default; orgs can enable auto-stamping via a feature flag at intake. The migration/backfill job writes affinity stamps as a separate commit and is reversible.

Testing/validation: unit tests for serializer, end-to-end integration with release-assembler in CI, a sampled backfill run capturing 500 variants for validation, and a small canary deploy of the compressed changelog to the docs pipeline.

Doc changes: docs/onboard/affinity-stamps.md, docs/release/changelog-compressor.md and a short console UI blurb to surface the top-3 affinity suggestions in the variant detail page.

Related: refers to ENG-4232 (affinity heuristics prototype) and ENG-4301 (changelog UX revamp).

source.title: Introduce staged intake intent and profile shadowing for safe, measurable model rollouts
source.author: Priya Natarajan
source.description: Context: As model intake velocity grows we need a lightweight safe path to stage new model manifests and performance profiles without impacting live serving. This PR adds a 'staged intake intent' concept plus 'profile shadowing' so teams can register candidate model versions, run sampled traffic/probes against shadowed profiles, and automatically synthesize a preview changelog describing measured regressions and cost delta. Motivation: reduce risk of blind rollouts, make onboarding telemetry-driven, and provide concise release-ready notes for model ops. Summary of features: 1) staged intake manifest schema (staged_intake.yaml) with intent metadata (owner, canary-criteria, probe-sets, retention-ttl); 2) profile-shadow runner service that can accept a staged manifest and shadow live traffic into a sandboxed profile evaluation pipeline (local kv-cache isolation + cost telemetry); 3) probe sampler that runs a configurable subset of promptsets (promptset refs in manifest) and computes key deltas (latency P95, tokens-per-request, quality-regression score) and stores a compact report; 4) changelog synthesizer that takes probe reports and generates a draft release blurb (short, medium, long) with links to artifacts; 5) lightweight web preview in registry UI (api route /staged-preview/:id) that renders the manifest + probe deltas + proposed release snippet. Implementation notes: aim for minimal runtime impact by making shadowing opt-in per manifest and sampling at route-level; identity-preserving telemetry tags are stripped before storage to meet compliance; manifests are immutable once marked "candidate", and shadow runs are tagged with provenance (uploader, commit, manifest-hash). Backwards compatibility: existing manifest ingestion remains unchanged unless a staged intent field is present. Checklist: - [x] manifest schema and validation - [x] profile-shadow runner and sandbox isolation - [x] probe sampler + three default promptsets - [x] changelog synthesizer with templating - [x] unit + integration tests for ingestion and draft generation - [x] docs/registry/staged-intake.md draft - [x] lightweight UI preview endpoint - [x] privacy/stripper for telemetry. Risks and mitigations: memory overhead during shadow runs mitigated by limiting concurrent shadow workers and reusing KV cache objects; behavioral regressions are surfaced only in preview and do not affect routing. Next steps: allow autoscheduling of shadow runs based on traffic thresholds, add RBAC controls for who can mark a manifest candidate, and wire preview generation to the release-notes exporter.
commits: a3f9b2e: add staged_intake manifest schema, validator and fixtures
b4d1e7c: implement profile-shadow runner, sandboxing primitives and memory caps
c2198f4: probe sampler and default promptsets + perf delta calculator
d55b1aa: changelog synthesizer and preview API route + UI stub
e1f8c0d: docs and tests: staged-intake.md, unit/integration tests and CI tweaks
files_changed: registry/schemas/staged_intake.yaml (new) - schema for staged manifests with canary and probe fields
registry/services/profile_shadow/runner.py - core shadow execution and kv-cache isolation
registry/api/staged_preview.py - read-only preview endpoint for manifests and probe reports
ui/src/pages/registry/StagedPreview.tsx - minimal preview page and example rendering
docs/registry/staged-intake.md - usage guide and safety notes
.github/workflows/ci.yaml - add smoke integration job for staged-preview (opt-in)
review_comments: Evan Zhao (2024-11-20): Nice pattern. Please add a provenance signature field on the manifest (uploader + commit) so we can trace who initiated a staged intent. Also add a TTL on probe artifacts to avoid storage bloat.
Priya Natarajan (2024-11-20): Added manifest.signature and probe_ttl in staged_intake.yaml and updated storage lifecycle. Reply: flagged in commit b4d1e7c.
María Gutiérrez (2024-11-21): Security note: ensure identity tags are stripped from telemetry before persisting. Can you add tests asserting that PII isn't saved?
Priya Natarajan (2024-11-21): Added telemetry_stripper util and unit tests (see e1f8c0d).

Alex Kim (2024-11-21): Observability: add explicit metrics for shadow_run_count, shadow_worker_memory, and probe_sample_rate. I also see one lint failure in runner.py regarding long method. Requested change: split runner._exec into smaller functions.
Priya Natarajan (2024-11-21): Split runner._exec into _prepare, _dispatch, _collect layers and added metrics. Pushed fix c2198f4. CI rerun requested.
ci_checks: unit-tests: failed (new test uncovered missing exception path) -> fixed in e1f8c0d; rerun passed
source.merge_outcome: merged via squash by María Gutiérrez on 2024-11-22 using squash merge; commit message consolidated
source.release_notes: Added staged intake manifests and profile shadowing to enable safe, observable model rollouts. Includes a probe sampler and changelog synthesizer that produces draft release notes with measured perf/quality deltas. Opt-in feature behind staged intent in manifests.
source.notes_for_operators: To enable preview runs in Dedicated clusters set registry.config.shadow_workers>0 and configure probe promptsets. Shadow runs are resource-limited; monitor metrics shadow_worker_memory and shadow_run_count. Use registry CLI 'registry preview publish <manifest>' to trigger manual runs.
source.qa_summary: QA ran a 48-hour shadow campaign against the prod-like perf-canary using three promptsets. Results were stable: latency delta <= 2ms P95 for 2/3 promptsets, quality score regression detected on one conversational promptset and surfaced in draft changelog. No leaks of identity tokens observed.

source.title: Introduce intake sentinel and brief compiler for compact changelogs
source.author: Alyssa Chen
source.description: Context: onboarding churn and long autogenerated release notes make it hard for engineers to quickly assess risk and performance impact of new model variants. This PR adds two cooperating subsystems: an intake-sentinel that runs lightweight sanity and cost/perf heuristics during model intake, and a brief-compiler that synthesizes compact, human-friendly changelog fragments and performance highlight cards. Motivation: reduce noise in release notes, accelerate review turnaround, and catch obvious regressions earlier in the pipeline. Scope: sentinel rules (schema checks, common-freeze tokens, latency/cost delta thresholds), telemetry-sampler hook (emits sampled perf traces for new variants), brief compiler (compact prose + delta table + example prompts), automation pipeline changes to attach compiled briefs to model uploads, and tests + migration. Checklist: - implement sentinel engine and rule registry; - add telemetry-sampler integration and sampling policy; - implement brief compiler with templating and delta heuristics; - attach compiled brief to model registry artifacts and release draft; - add unit + e2e tests and CI job; - docs stub for reviewer workflow. Impact: aims to be backward-compatible with existing onboard flows; sentinel blocks uploads only when high-confidence critical violations are detected (configurable); compiled briefs are optional by feature flag. Related: ENG-2173 (onboard quality gates), ENG-2201 (changelog noise reduction).
commits: 72c9b4d add sentinel core and rule registry
9a4f2e1 wire telemetry sampler into intake pipeline
1e0b3d7 add brief compiler and templates
3c2d5f0 attach compiled brief to model artifact metadata
5f7a8a1 tests: sentinel unit tests and e2e intake smoke test
b1d2c54 docs: onboarding reviewer guide (draft)
review_thread: Diego Morales: Please clarify the threshold logic for latency regressions; is it absolute or relative?
Alyssa Chen: It's relative by default (pct delta over baseline) with an override for absolute ms in config; added comment and unit test to cover both paths.
Priya Natarajan: The sampled telemetry may add cost—can we cap sample rate per-org?
Alyssa Chen: Yes, sample rate is org-scoped and defaults to 0.5% for new variants; rate is configurable via infra flags; noted in docs.
Tomás Silva: Brief templating looks good but include a terse machine-readable summary for automated rollouts.
Alyssa Chen: Added a JSON summary blob alongside the human brief (brief.summary.json) for rollouts and A/B tooling.
Priya Natarajan: Approving pending a green E2E run.
CI: onboard-e2e passed on rerun; lint and unit tests green.
source.merge_outcome: merged (squash) into main after CI green; release draft artifact includes compiled brief and sampled telemetry links.
source.release_notes: Adds an intake sentinel that enforces lightweight sanity and perf heuristics during model onboarding and a brief compiler that synthesizes compact changelog fragments and perf highlight cards. Config: sentinel rules and telemetry sampling are configurable per-org. Migration: no forced data migration; new briefs appear when feature flag enabled.
source.notes_for_ops: Feature disabled by default; rollout plan: enable for internal onboarding namespace for 2 weeks, monitor noise and blocked uploads, then enable opt-in for customers. Ops should watch telemetry.sampled_rate and sentinel.block_rate metrics.
source.security_considerations: Sentinel performs schema and token checks only; no model weights or PII are stored by new components. Telemetry sampler respects PII redaction and follows existing telemetry retention policies.
source.testing_summary: Unit tests cover rule evaluator edge cases and template rendering; e2e smoke simulates a model intake with synthetic perf traces and verifies compiled brief attachment. CI adds an on-demand onboard-e2e job that runs in the PR pipeline.
source.long_term_roadmap: Follow-ups: add automated severity scoring for changelog snippets, integrate with release-brief web UI, and expose brief diffing in model history timeline.

source.title: Introduce lifecycle signatures and staged compatibility snippets for onboarding changelogs
source.author: Priya Shah
source.description:
Background: onboarding churn and subtle compatibility regressions have made changelogs noisy and hard to consume. We need a small, machine-readable lifecycle signal attached to model profiles so release automation can produce concise, actionable changelog snippets when a model moves between lifecycle phases (onboard -> staging -> prod). This PR introduces 'lifecycle_signature' metadata on model profiles and a staged compatibility-snippet generator that outputs human-friendly changelog fragments and suggested fallback mappings for deployments.

What this PR does: introduces a new lightweight lifecycle_signature schema (versioned), adds an onboarding hook to compute the signature, adds a changelog assembler plugin that emits staged compatibility snippets, and wires a CLI flag to preview generated snippets. The generator uses a deterministic fingerprint of (architecture, tokenizer_hash, primary_quant, perf_token_summary) and a short delta heuristic to summarize compatibility impact and performance deltas.

Checklist:
- [x] lifecycle_signature schema and migration support
- [x] onboarding hook: registry/hooks/lifecycle_signer.py
- [x] changelog assembler plugin: registry/hooks/compat_snippets.py
- [x] CLI preview: registry/cli/onboard_preview.py --staged-snippets
- [x] unit tests for signature calculation and snippet heuristics
- [x] docs: docs/onboard-lifecycle.md with examples and rollout guidance

Design notes: the lifecycle_signature is intentionally compact (hex-encoded 12 bytes + version tag) to remain stable in manifests and to avoid bloating release bundles. The staged compatibility snippet is a short paragraph and a suggested remap table (previous_alias -> recommended_variant) to help platform teams stage rollouts. The heuristics aim for conservative suggestions (only propose remaps when the signature delta + perf delta exceed thresholds).

Why this is different from profile hashes: profile hashes are content fingerprints; lifecycle_signature encodes a small set of compatibility-relevant attributes and a stability score to be used by release tooling and console UI for clearer onboarding narratives.

Backward compatibility and migration: existing profiles without lifecycle_signature are treated as 'unknown' and will get a computed signature on the next onboard. The migration is non-blocking; the assembler falls back to legacy blurb generation if signature is absent.

Operational considerations: the plugin adds a single CPU-bound computation during onboarding and a fast, cached path in changelog assembly. We added a short TTL cache keyed by model_id+head_sha to avoid recomputation for large batch uploads.

Risks and mitigations: initial heuristics may be conservative and under-suggest remaps; we added a monitoring metric (registry.lifecycle.signer.suggestions.count) and a small sampling telemetry to evaluate precision.

How to test locally: run `pytest tests/test_lifecycle_signatures.py::test_signature_determinism` and `python -m registry.cli.onboard_preview --file sample_profile.json --staged-snippets` to see generated snippet output.

Related: ENG-7421 (project to tighten onboarding churn), PM-318 (product request for clearer release notes).
commits: 7a3b2c1 - add lifecycle_signature schema and serializer (registry/schema.py, registry/serializers.py)
c2f4e9a - implement onboarding signer hook and TTL cache (registry/hooks/lifecycle_signer.py, registry/cli/onboard_preview.py)
b1d9f34 - add changelog assembler plugin for staged compatibility snippets and tests (registry/hooks/compat_snippets.py, tests/test_lifecycle_signatures.py)
d4e6a70 - docs and examples for staged snippets (docs/onboard-lifecycle.md, examples/onboard-snippet-samples.json)
review_comments: Mateo Alvarez: Nice approach on keeping the signature compact. Can we document the exact field order used for the fingerprint? Requested change: add a unit test to assert stable ordering so CI catches accidental reorderings.
Priya Shah: Added tests/test_lifecycle_signatures.py::test_field_ordering and a serialization assertion. Also expanded comments in registry/serializers.py to call out ordering.
Janelle Kwan: LGTm overall, one nit: the CLI flag name is a little verbose. Can we alias --staged-snippets with --preview-snippets?
Priya Shah: Added alias --preview-snippets and updated docs. Updated tests to call preview code path.
Owen Price: CI flaked on the first run (integration timeout). Re-ran checks; all green. I approve pending squash merge.
Janelle Kwan: Approved after the alias change and small docs tweak.
Release Manager (bot): merge ready, release_notes field populated.
source.release_notes: Add lifecycle_signature metadata and a changelog plugin that generates staged compatibility snippets for onboarded models. This enables concise, machine-friendly release notes and suggested variant remaps during rollouts. Includes a CLI preview and tests.
